title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.

Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. - In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Summary

This paper presents a novel algorithm for constructing Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) prediction sets in the context of meta-learning. The authors aim to adapt a predictor to new tasks with only a few training examples while providing rigorous guarantees on the correctness of the predictions. The proposed algorithm, Meta-PS, extends the PAC prediction set framework to the meta-learning setting, offering high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

The paper is well-written, and the experiments are interesting. The authors do well in pointing out that their PAC calibration framework offers several desirable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. This seems like a meaningful contribution, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks.

One of the main strengths of the paper is its theoretical setting, which is appealing and a reasonable extension to Meta-CP. The calibration strategy seems correct and well-presented. The empirical results also seem strong, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets and a realistic heart attack prediction task.

However, there are a few weaknesses. One concern is that a single threshold is used for all downstream tasks, i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to estimate a data-dependent threshold. This could be better in terms of being test-task conditionally, especially when there is enough few-shot data or significant variability across tasks.

Another potential weakness is the use of Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes. The authors assume access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks, which might be unrealistic in certain scenarios. While the theory is technically solid, it is a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration).

## Readability and Presentation

The notation in the paper is quite burdensome, which can make it difficult to follow. Providing a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it would be helpful. For example, a clear explanation of the goals of Meta-PAC sets, such as finding a single, shared threshold to use that satisfies certain conditions, would be beneficial.

## Experiments and Results

The experiments seem well-designed, and the results are interesting. The authors evaluate their algorithm on several datasets, including mini-ImageNet, FewRel, and the CDC Heart Dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm satisfies the meta PAC criterion and produces smaller prediction sets compared to other approaches.

## Limitations and Potential Impact

The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. However, it might be worth expanding on the limitations of the current approach, such as requiring enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee. Additionally, it is essential to note that the proposed algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation but rather one that relies on several strong assumptions and only holds on average across new tasks.

## Decision

Rating: 6: Weak Accept

The paper is technically solid, and the experiments are well-designed. The proposed algorithm offers desirable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. While there are some weaknesses, such as the use of a single threshold for all downstream tasks and the reliance on small calibration task sizes, the paper presents a meaningful contribution to the field of meta-learning and PAC prediction sets. The decision to weakly accept the paper is well-supported by the review.

## Additional Comments

* The authors may want to consider revising the notation to make it more accessible to readers.
* Providing a high-level description of the algorithm and its goals would be helpful in making the paper more readable.
* The limitations of the current approach should be further discussed, and the potential impact of the proposed algorithm should be clarified.

## Expanded Review

The paper proposes a novel algorithm for constructing PAC prediction sets in the context of meta-learning. The authors aim to adapt a predictor to new tasks with only a few training examples while providing rigorous guarantees on the correctness of the predictions. The proposed algorithm, Meta-PS, extends the PAC prediction set framework to the meta-learning setting, offering high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks.

One of the main strengths of the paper is its theoretical setting, which is appealing and a reasonable extension to Meta-CP. The calibration strategy seems correct and well-presented. The empirical results also seem strong, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets and a realistic heart attack prediction task.

However, there are a few weaknesses. One concern is that a single threshold is used for all downstream tasks, i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to estimate a data-dependent threshold. This could be better in terms of being test-task conditionally, especially when there is enough few-shot data or significant variability across tasks.

Another potential weakness is the use of Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes. The authors assume access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks, which might be unrealistic in certain scenarios. While the theory is technically solid, it is a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration).

The experiments seem well-designed, and the results are interesting. The authors evaluate their algorithm on several datasets, including mini-ImageNet, FewRel, and the CDC Heart Dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm satisfies the meta PAC criterion and produces smaller prediction sets compared to other approaches.

The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. However, it might be worth expanding on the limitations of the current approach, such as requiring enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee. Additionally, it is essential to note that the proposed algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation but rather one that relies on several strong assumptions and only holds on average across new tasks.

Overall, the paper presents a meaningful contribution to the field of meta-learning and PAC prediction sets. The decision to weakly accept the paper is well-supported by the review.

## References

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

## Checklist

1. For all authors...
(a) Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper’s contributions and scope? [Yes]
(b) Did you describe the limitations of your work? [Yes] See Section 6.
(c) Did you discuss any potential negative societal impacts of your work? [Yes] See Section 6
(d) Have you read the ethics review guidelines and ensured that your paper conforms to them? [Yes]

2. If you are including theoretical results...
(a) Did you state the full set of assumptions of all theoretical results? [Yes] See Section 4.1.
(b) Did you include complete proofs of all theoretical results? [Yes] See Section C.

3. If you ran experiments...
(a) Did you include the code, data, and instructions needed to reproduce the main experimental results (either in the supplemental material or as a URL)? [No] Code will be released once accepted along with data and precise instructions to run it.
(b) Did you specify all the training details (e.g., data splits, hyperparameters, how they were chosen)? [Yes] See Section 5 and D.
(c) Did you report error bars (e.g., with respect to the random seed after running experiments multiple times)? [Yes] We use box plots to represent the randomness on error.
(d) Did you include the total amount of compute and the type of resources used (e.g., type of GPUs, internal cluster, or cloud provider)? [Yes] See Section D.1.

4. If you are using existing assets (e.g., code, data, models) or curating/releasing new assets...
(a) If your work uses existing assets, did you cite the creators? [Yes]
(b) Did you mention the license of the assets? [Yes] See Section D.2.
(c) Did you include any new assets either in the supplemental material or as a URL? [No]
(d) Did you discuss whether and how consent was obtained from people whose data you’re using/curating? [Yes] See Section D.2.
(e) Did you discuss whether the data you are using/curating contains personally identifiable information or offensive content? [Yes] See Section D.2.

5. If you used crowdsourcing or conducted research with human subjects...
(a) Did you include the full text of instructions given to participants and screenshots, if applicable? [N/A]
(b) Did you describe any potential participant risks, with links to Institutional Review Board (IRB) approvals, if applicable? [N/A]
(c) Did you include the estimated hourly wage paid to participants and the total amount spent on participant compensation? [N/A]

This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Weakness:

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning, which adapts a predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. The proposed method demonstrates effectiveness across four datasets, satisfying a PAC guarantee while achieving smaller prediction set sizes.

Strengths and Weaknesses:
Strengths:

* The paper is well-written, presenting a clear and concise overview of the proposed method and its theoretical underpinnings.
* The proposed method is reasonable, with a thorough theoretical analysis that provides a solid foundation for the approach.
* The experiments conducted on various domains and datasets are comprehensive, providing a robust evaluation of the proposed method.

Weaknesses:

* One potential limitation of the proposed approach is that it reuses the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation. It would be beneficial to explore adapting the meta prediction set parameters as well, potentially leading to improved performance.
* The current method appears to be primarily applicable to metric-based meta-learning methods, such as prototypical networks. Extending the approach to accommodate optimization-based methods, like model-agnostic meta learning (MAML), would be an interesting direction for future research.
* For the mini-ImageNet dataset, the experiments are limited to 5-way classification problems. Evaluating the proposed method on more challenging tasks, such as 10-way or 20-way classification, would provide a more comprehensive understanding of its capabilities.

Questions:
Please refer to the weaknesses mentioned above.

Limitations:
The authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work, providing a clear discussion of the assumptions and potential constraints of the proposed approach.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The proposed PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning is a valuable contribution to the field, offering a theoretically grounded approach to adapting predictors to new tasks with limited labeled data. The experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the method across various domains and datasets, and the authors provide a clear discussion of the limitations and potential impacts of their work.

To further strengthen the paper, it would be beneficial to explore the adaptation of meta prediction set parameters, as well as the application of the proposed method to optimization-based meta-learning approaches. Additionally, evaluating the method on more challenging tasks, such as higher-way classification problems, would provide a more comprehensive understanding of its capabilities.

Overall, the paper is well-written, and the proposed method is reasonable and theoretically well-founded. The experiments are comprehensive, and the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. Therefore, I recommend a Weak Accept rating, indicating a technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper with no major concerns. 

The original review provided a good summary of the paper and identified some weaknesses. However, I will add more details and examples to support the points.

One of the strengths of the paper is that it provides a thorough theoretical analysis of the proposed method. The authors have done a great job of explaining the PAC guarantee and how it applies to the meta learning setting. They have also provided a clear discussion of the related work, including the connections to conformal prediction and meta conformal prediction.

The experiments are comprehensive, and the authors have provided a clear discussion of the setup and the results. The proposed method is compared to several baselines, including PS, PS-Test, and Meta-CP, and it performs well in terms of prediction set error and size.

However, as mentioned earlier, there are some weaknesses that need to be addressed. The proposed method reuses the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, which might not be optimal. It would be beneficial to explore adapting the meta prediction set parameters as well.

Additionally, the current method appears to be primarily applicable to metric-based meta-learning methods, such as prototypical networks. Extending the approach to accommodate optimization-based methods, like model-agnostic meta learning (MAML), would be an interesting direction for future research.

In terms of the limitations, the authors have discussed the potential negative societal impact of their work, including the possibility of misuse. They have also discussed the limitations of the current approach, including the requirement for enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee.

Overall, I agree with the original review that the paper is technically solid and has a moderate-to-high impact. The proposed method is reasonable, and the experiments are comprehensive. However, there are some weaknesses that need to be addressed, and I recommend a Weak Accept rating.

The expanded review maintains the same decision/rating as the original review while providing more details and examples to support the points. The new content is integrated seamlessly, and the review reads naturally and fluently. 

The review also preserves all the original critique points and adds new observations, providing a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed method. The use of specific details from the manuscript supports the points made in the review, and the tone is maintained throughout.

In conclusion, the expanded review provides a thorough evaluation of the proposed PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses. The recommendations provided will help strengthen the paper, and I recommend a Weak Accept rating.

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

## Review

The paper "PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning" proposes a novel algorithm for constructing Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. The authors aim to provide a rigorous guarantee for prediction sets in the meta-learning setting, where the goal is to quickly adapt a predictor to new tasks with only a few training examples.

**Summary of Original Critique Points**

The original review praised the paper for its technical soundness, clear theoretical analysis, and significance in providing insights into conformal prediction in the meta-learning setting. However, the reviewer also raised a question about the computational cost and efficiency of the grid search involved in Algorithm 2, specifically asking about the granularity of the search.

**Expanded Review**

The paper makes significant contributions to the field of meta-learning and conformal prediction. The proposed algorithm, Meta-PS, constructs PAC prediction sets that capture uncertainty via sets of labels and can be adapted to new tasks with only a few training examples. The authors demonstrate the efficacy of their approach on four datasets across three application domains: mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C in the visual domain, FewRel in the language domain, and the CDC Heart Dataset in the medical domain.

One of the key strengths of the paper is its technical soundness. The authors provide a clear and rigorous theoretical analysis of the proposed algorithm, including a proof of the meta-PAC guarantee (Theorem 1). The algorithm is also well-motivated, and the authors provide a clear explanation of the problem setup and the proposed solution.

However, as mentioned in the original review, there is a potential concern about the computational cost and efficiency of the grid search involved in Algorithm 2. The authors could provide more details on the granularity of the search and explore possible ways to accelerate the grid search. For instance, they could investigate the use of more efficient optimization algorithms or approximation techniques to reduce the computational cost.

The paper is well-written and easy to follow, making it accessible to a wide range of readers. The authors provide a clear explanation of the related work and the contributions of the paper. The experimental results are also well-presented, and the authors demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach on several benchmark datasets.

**Additional Observations**

The paper highlights the importance of considering multiple sources of randomness in the meta-learning setting, including the adaptation set, calibration set, and test data. The authors provide a clear explanation of how their approach controls these sources of randomness and error.

The proposed algorithm has a potential societal impact, as it can be used in safety-critical systems such as healthcare and autonomous vehicles. However, the authors also discuss potential limitations and negative societal impacts, such as the requirement for enough

Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

Summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Figure 1: An illustration of our meta-learning framework for constructing PAC prediction sets. We
learn a meta-learner (top) using task-datasets p✓1 and p✓2 . For each task, we construct adaptation
sets A1 and A2 , and calibration sets. We then adapt the meta-learner on adaptation sets to get
task-speciﬁc predictors f A1 and f A2 . These predictors are calibrated on the calibration sets to get
calibrated predictors ⌧1 and ⌧2 . For inference on a new task (bottom), we adapt the meta-learner on
an adaptation set A , and calibrate on a calibration set to get a task-speciﬁc calibrated predictor ⌧ .

## Original Peer Review

The authors propose a novel algorithm to construct PAC prediction sets for the meta-learning setting. The proposed approach adapts to new tasks with a few training examples and provides a PAC guarantee that holds with high probability over future tasks. The authors demonstrate the efficacy of their approach on four datasets across three application domains. The manuscript is well-written and easy to follow. However, there are several areas that need improvement. 

The introduction could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the existing methods for constructing prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. The authors mention that existing methods do not provide guarantees that hold with high probability over future tasks, but they do not provide a thorough analysis of these methods and their limitations. 

The proposed algorithm is not thoroughly analyzed. The authors provide a high-level overview of their approach but do not provide a detailed analysis of its components. For instance, the role of calibration sets in their approach is not clearly explained. The authors should provide more details on how the calibration sets are constructed and how they contribute to the PAC guarantee. 

The experimental evaluation is limited. The authors only evaluate their approach on four datasets across three domains. While the results are promising, the authors should evaluate their approach on more datasets to demonstrate its efficacy. 

The manuscript could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the implications of the PAC guarantee in the meta-learning setting. The authors mention that the PAC guarantee holds with high probability over future tasks, but they do not provide a thorough analysis of what this means in practice. 

The authors should provide more details on the potential applications of their approach. While the authors mention a few applications, they do not provide a thorough analysis of how their approach can be used in these domains. 

## Expanded Peer Review

The authors propose a novel algorithm to construct PAC prediction sets for the meta-learning setting. The proposed approach adapts to new tasks with a few training examples and provides a PAC guarantee that holds with high probability over future tasks. The authors demonstrate the efficacy of their approach on four datasets across three application domains. The manuscript is well-written and easy to follow. However, there are several areas that need improvement.

The introduction could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the existing methods for constructing prediction sets in the meta-learning setting. The authors mention that existing methods do not provide guarantees that hold with high probability over future tasks, but they do not provide a thorough analysis of these methods and their limitations. For instance, the authors could discuss the work of [14] in more detail, explaining how their approach differs from and improves upon existing methods. Specifically, the authors could elaborate on how their approach addresses the limitations of existing methods, such as the lack of guarantees that hold with high probability over future tasks.

The proposed algorithm is not thoroughly analyzed. The authors provide a high-level overview of their approach but do not provide a detailed analysis of its components. For instance, the role of calibration sets in their approach is not clearly explained. The authors should provide more details on how the calibration sets are constructed and how they contribute to the PAC guarantee. As shown in Figure 1, the calibration sets are used to calibrate the task-specific predictors, but the authors do not provide a clear explanation of how this calibration process works. A more detailed explanation of the calibration process and its importance in ensuring the PAC guarantee would strengthen the manuscript.

The experimental evaluation is limited. The authors only evaluate their approach on four datasets across three domains. While the results are promising, the authors should evaluate their approach on more datasets to demonstrate its efficacy. For example, the authors could evaluate their approach on additional datasets in the visual domain, such as CIFAR-100 or ImageNet-1K. Moreover, the authors could provide more details on the experimental setup, such as the specific hyperparameters used and the computational resources required. This would provide a clearer understanding of the experimental evaluation and make the results more reproducible.

The manuscript could benefit from a more detailed discussion on the implications of the PAC guarantee in the meta-learning setting. The authors mention that the PAC guarantee holds with high probability over future tasks, but they do not provide a thorough analysis of what this means in practice. For instance, the authors could discuss how the PAC guarantee provides a bound on the error rate of the predictor on future tasks, and how this bound relates to the number of training examples used for adaptation. As mentioned in the manuscript, the PAC guarantee is an extension of the typical PAC guarantee to the meta-learning setting, and the authors could provide more details on how this extension is achieved.

The authors should provide more details on the potential applications of their approach. While the authors mention a few applications, such as autonomous driving and healthcare, they do not provide a thorough analysis of how their approach can be used in these domains. For example, the authors could discuss how their approach can be used to improve the safety and reliability of autonomous driving systems, or how it can be used to provide more accurate predictions in healthcare. Specifically, the authors could elaborate on how their approach can be used to address specific challenges in these domains, such as handling out-of-distribution data or providing transparent and interpretable predictions.

Furthermore, the authors could provide more details on the theoretical guarantees provided by their approach. While the manuscript mentions that the PAC guarantee holds with high probability over future tasks, the authors do not provide a detailed analysis of the theoretical implications of this guarantee. For instance, the authors could discuss how the PAC guarantee relates to other theoretical guarantees in the meta-learning setting, such as the Probably Approximately Correct (PAC)-Bayes guarantee.

In conclusion, while the manuscript proposes a novel and promising approach to constructing PAC prediction sets for the meta-learning setting, there are several areas that need improvement. The authors should provide more details on the existing methods for constructing prediction sets in the meta-learning setting, the role of calibration sets in their approach, and the implications of the PAC guarantee in practice. Additionally, the authors should evaluate their approach on more datasets and provide more details on the potential applications of their approach.

Based on the current manuscript, I recommend **Major Revisions**. The authors have presented a promising approach, but the manuscript requires significant improvements to be considered for publication. 

Decision: **Major Revisions** 

Rating: **6** 

Recommendation: The authors should address the above-mentioned concerns and provide a revised manuscript that thoroughly addresses these concerns.

It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
The paper proposes a method for estimating prediction sets in the meta-test stage, gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The proposed method is guaranteed by the PAC bound, which provides a theoretical guarantee for generating prediction sets.

One of the strengths of the proposed method is that it provides a PAC guarantee, which ensures that the prediction sets are valid and reliable. However, the paper could benefit from a clearer explanation of the notation and the background of the proposed method.

The paper assumes a certain level of familiarity with the concepts of meta-learning and prediction sets, which may make it difficult for readers who are not experts in the field to understand. To improve clarity, the authors could provide more background information on the concepts and notation used in the paper.

The proposed method is evaluated on four datasets across three application domains, and the results show that it performs competitively. However, the paper could benefit from a more detailed analysis of the results, including a comparison with other methods.

One of the limitations of the paper is that it uses a single score function, and it is not clear whether this is the best approach. The authors could explore using multiple score functions and evaluating their performance.

The paper also assumes that the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases are well-matched to the meta-test stage, which may not always be the case. The authors could explore the sensitivity of the proposed method to mismatches between the train and test distributions.

Despite these limitations, the proposed method shows promise, and the authors provide a thorough evaluation of its performance. The paper could benefit from additional analysis and experimentation to further validate the proposed method.

In terms of the notation, the paper could benefit from a clear and concise explanation of the symbols and notation used throughout the paper. This would make it easier for readers to understand the paper and its contributions.

Overall, the paper proposes a promising method for estimating prediction sets in the meta-test stage, and the authors provide a thorough evaluation of its performance. However, the paper could benefit from additional analysis and experimentation to further validate the proposed method.

The proposed method has a number of potential applications, including meta-learning, transfer learning, and few-shot learning. The authors could explore these applications in more detail and discuss the potential benefits and limitations of the proposed method.

In conclusion, the paper proposes a promising method for estimating prediction sets in the meta-test stage, and the authors provide a thorough evaluation of its performance. However, the paper could benefit from additional analysis and experimentation to further validate the proposed method.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.
reviews.questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. reviews.text:
Summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning, which involves training a conditional RL policy that can effectively respond to high-level commands. The authors propose a constrained RL algorithm, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to train an agent capable of winning strategy games while following most human instructions. RED combines RL to learn exploratory policies with Behavior Cloning (BC) to imitate human demonstrations.

The experiment results indicate that RED achieves command-following policies and a higher winning rate compared to baselines.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise: utilizing BC to learn human behavior while leveraging RL to explore better solutions is a straightforward yet effective approach.
* The provided visualizations and videos are mostly clear and contain necessary information, helping to understand the concept of grounded reinforcement learning.
* Figure 1 is illustrative and helpful in explaining grounded reinforcement learning; however, it would be better if it were in PDF format without background color.
* The code is released, which is a significant plus for reproducibility.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes that rewards are available from the interactive environment, which is a limitation.
* The code lacks detailed guidance and documentation, making it difficult for others to understand and reproduce the results.
* Although various experiments are conducted, the main experiment results in Table 1 are confusing and not entirely convincing.
* The idea of combining BC and RL is not novel; it has been explored in previous works such as "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning."
* Several details are missing or confusing, such as the explanation of Table 1 and the Lagrangian method used.

Questions:
### Questions on method

The use of the Lagrangian multiplier in Sec 4.3 is not entirely clear. The paper mentions using it to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq 2, but it appears to be simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should include an update process for $\beta$, which seems to be missing. The later "iterative solution" also deviates from the traditional Lagrangian method.

Furthermore, the action space handling is not clearly explained, particularly the "0/1 action for each unit." Clarification on how this selection is made and who performs the selection would be helpful.

The insertion of a "NA" command is also not well-explained. If there is a fixed set of possible commands, it is assumed that "NA" is just another command in that set. The process of creating an embedding for each command and passing it to the neural network needs further clarification.

### Questions on experiments

Table 1 presents the main experiment results, but it is challenging to understand why the baselines, which do not take commands as input, have different performances under various test commands. Clarification on how these baselines are turned into conditional policies is necessary.

For instance, the RL policy is trained without any objective to produce different behaviors under different commands. Therefore, it is unclear why different test commands lead to different behaviors.

The results in Table 1 are also not convincing, especially when considering the standard deviation. The statement that "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands" is overclaimed and not directly supported by the data in Table 1.

Figure 2, on the other hand, is more convincing as it shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. It demonstrates that RED has a much better "causality" between the input command and the behavior than RL. However, there are some inconsistencies between Figure 2 and Table 1 regarding the win rate of RL under different conditions.

Moreover, the human evaluation results in Figure 6 indicate that the RL policy achieves a similar win rate compared to RED, despite RL not being conditioned on human commands. This seems to contradict the results in Table 1 under the "Oracle" condition.

The provided gameplay footage is limited, with only 6 commands given over less than 2 minutes, which is not convincing. The commands appear to be programmed or copy-pasted without any input lag, raising questions about the involvement of real human intervention.

The complexity of the game environment raises questions about whether the algorithm was initially intended for such a high-level game. Exploring alternative environments, even if the performance is weak, could add diversity to the paper.

### Questions on paper

The Google Drive link provided for additional resources is not anonymous, violating the NeurIPS Code of Conduct. The authors should take immediate action to address this.

The paper includes experiments involving 30 college students, but it lacks an explicit Ethics Statement regarding the participants' awareness of their rights and responsibilities.

The idea of conditional BC is not new and has been explored in previous works. The appendix contains some necessary information but is not comprehensive, particularly regarding human experiments, which were moved to Appendix E.

The website provided in the appendix is informative, with real-time gameplay footage. However, there are issues such as a broken code link and numerous grammar errors.

Limitations:
The authors provide a limitation section, discussing potential future directions such as improving human-AI cooperation, learning a better human proxy, and quantifying the causality between commands and behaviors.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

To further support the decision, additional analysis and clarification on the raised questions are necessary. The authors should provide more details on their methodology, especially regarding the Lagrangian method and action space handling. Moreover, addressing the concerns about the experiment results and providing more convincing evidence, such as more extensive gameplay footage involving real human intervention, would strengthen the paper.

The expansion of the review maintains the same overall tone and level of detail as the original while preserving all the core critique points. The decision to rate the paper as "Weak Accept" remains unchanged, as the expanded review supports the same acceptance recommendation.

Additional Context and Related Observations:

Upon further examination, it becomes clear that the RED algorithm's performance is heavily dependent on the quality of the human demonstrations and the design of the command-following metric. The use of an LSTM encoder to handle arbitrary natural language commands is a reasonable approach, but its sensitivity to input words and word order, as shown in Table 3, highlights a potential vulnerability.

The ablation study in Section 5.3 provides valuable insights into the importance of the ratio between on-policy samples and demonstrations. The finding that a 1:1 ratio leads to a sufficiently good policy is useful, but further exploration of this hyperparameter could provide more robust results.

The human evaluation results, while interesting, raise questions about the participants' familiarity with the game and the policies. A more detailed analysis of the human evaluation process and the participants' feedback could offer additional insights into the command-following abilities of the RED policy.

In conclusion, while the RED algorithm shows promise in achieving command-following policies with high win rates, further clarification and analysis are necessary to fully support the claims made in the paper. Addressing the concerns raised in this review could lead to a more robust and convincing presentation of the research.

Rating: 6 (Weak Accept)
