reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper takes on the challenge of both playing a game as optimally as possible, while following instructions and learning from demonstrations (both of which may be sub-optimal). The paper formulates this problem as a joint optimization, and tackles it by alternating steps of RL training with steps of distilling both demonstrations and successful RL trajectories. The paper evaluates this approach on the MiniRTS game, and evaluates against a range of baselines on various aspects such as rate of winning under different command types, how humans rank the policy’s instruction following, etc.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Post-response update
-----------

The authors have addressed some of my main concerns, and more thoroughly evaluated their method. I believe these new experiments could be better integrated in to the main text, and the paper is still limited by experimenting only with a single environment, but I am increasing my score in accordance with the improvements.



Original review
--------------


Strengths:
* An interesting and well-motivated problem space and proposed methods.
* In-depth experiments evaluating different perspectives on the issues raised in one domain.
* Good ablations and baseline conditions.
* Generally well written and clear. 

Weaknesses:
*The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not directly supported, only through circumstantial evidence that (while helpful) is not as direct as it could be.
  - The fact that win rates decrease with random commands is very weak evidence that the commands are being followed. The fact that humans rank the RED policy slightly higher than the rest is a little better, but the effect is weak (as noted in the paper there is an even distribution over the first rank). The statistical significance of the effect is not quantified, but there would almost certainly not be a significant difference between RED and the Switch or Joint baselines. 
  - To address this concern, it would be ideal to actually evaluate how well the agent follows commands. For example, set up a situation like Fig. 1E, give the agent a command like “Retreat”, and then evaluate whether the agent actually moves its units away or simply continues attacking, and how long it maintains this behavior. This would be far stronger, direct evidence that the agent is actually following commands (rather than e.g. doing nothing, issuing resource gathering commands without actually retreating, etc.).
  - Performing these tests in *precisely* situations where the RL policy would do something very different would be the strongest test of behavior.
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know whether the conclusions are generalizable; it would be ideal to perform experiments in 1-2 other settings as well.
* The general approach to the prior literature could be improved. For example:
  - The paper argues that “BC and IRL algorithms assume that the demonstrations are optimal”—however, a variety of prior works have addressed this problem in various ways, both from the perspective of IRL (e.g. https://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/brown19a.html) and from BC-like approaches conditioned on rewards (https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.02875), or just by using BC to initialize RL training (e.g., https://www.nature.com/articles/nature16961). While not all these perspectives would necessarily address the issue of also following commands, they are not incompatible with it, and the paper should discuss how such approaches might relate to (or complement) the proposed technique.
  - Relatedly, prior work on agents that follow human instructions (particularly https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.05672) has relied on a combination of BC, RL, and IRL to achieve better performance than BC alone; this would be useful to discuss.
* It seems to me that there is a fundamental tension between the goals of strictly following human commands, and winning the game. The proposed approach appears to pursue a policy that effectively tries to distort all human commands towards its own objective (i.e., to follow the command in the way that is most likely to lead to winning, as in Fig. 1C vs. 1D). Phrased in this way, it seems that there are clear alignment concerns about this approach. These should be discussed as a tradeoff/risk.
  - These concerns relate to the above questions about how well the agent is following commands—if the goal is for the agent to actually strictly follow human commands, it should not e.g. follow a retreat order for one second and then go right back to attacking.
  - It seems to me that it would be *far* safer to have a system that follows human commands first and foremost—i.e., a system that is “interruptable” (https://deepmindsafetyresearch.medium.com/building-safe-artificial-intelligence-52f5f75058f1 and https://www.ijcai.org/Proceedings/2017/0032.pdf)
  - This concern is *somewhat* ameliorated by the fact that the win rates under sub-optimal commands are much lower, but it depends on how frequently the commands are issued in these conditions.
  - Regardless, these issues should be discussed.
reviews.questions:
See above, but briefly:
* Could the authors demonstrate more directly how well the agents follow commands—particularly in cases where they commands are strongly divergent from the winning policy?
* What do the authors think might be the risks of the proposed algorithm?
reviews.limitations:
On the whole, I think this paper could be a useful contribution. The most important changes from my perspective would be (see above for full details):
* The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not particularly well supported. Experiments on how well the agents are actually following commands would be most important for improving my rating.
* There are risks to trying to minimally follow commands while pursuing another objective. Discussing these shouldn't be a major change.

Other limitations that I don't think are as critical to address, but would be helpful:
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know how general the advantage is.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper takes on the challenge of both playing a game as optimally as possible, while following instructions and learning from demonstrations (both of which may be sub-optimal). The paper formulates this problem as a joint optimization, and tackles it by alternating steps of RL training with steps of distilling both demonstrations and successful RL trajectories. The paper evaluates this approach on the MiniRTS game, and evaluates against a range of baselines on various aspects such as rate of winning under different command types, how humans rank the policy’s instruction following, etc.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Post-response update
-----------

The authors have addressed some of my main concerns, and more thoroughly evaluated their method. I believe these new experiments could be better integrated in to the main text, and the paper is still limited by experimenting only with a single environment, but I am increasing my score in accordance with the improvements.



Original review
--------------


Strengths:
* An interesting and well-motivated problem space and proposed methods.
* In-depth experiments evaluating different perspectives on the issues raised in one domain.
* Good ablations and baseline conditions.
* Generally well written and clear. 

Weaknesses:
*The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not directly supported, only through circumstantial evidence that (while helpful) is not as direct as it could be.
  - The fact that win rates decrease with random commands is very weak evidence that the commands are being followed. The fact that humans rank the RED policy slightly higher than the rest is a little better, but the effect is weak (as noted in the paper there is an even distribution over the first rank). The statistical significance of the effect is not quantified, but there would almost certainly not be a significant difference between RED and the Switch or Joint baselines. 
  - To address this concern, it would be ideal to actually evaluate how well the agent follows commands. For example, set up a situation like Fig. 1E, give the agent a command like “Retreat”, and then evaluate whether the agent actually moves its units away or simply continues attacking, and how long it maintains this behavior. This would be far stronger, direct evidence that the agent is actually following commands (rather than e.g. doing nothing, issuing resource gathering commands without actually retreating, etc.).
  - Performing these tests in *precisely* situations where the RL policy would do something very different would be the strongest test of behavior.
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know whether the conclusions are generalizable; it would be ideal to perform experiments in 1-2 other settings as well.
* The general approach to the prior literature could be improved. For example:
  - The paper argues that “BC and IRL algorithms assume that the demonstrations are optimal”—however, a variety of prior works have addressed this problem in various ways, both from the perspective of IRL (e.g. https://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/brown19a.html) and from BC-like approaches conditioned on rewards (https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.02875), or just by using BC to initialize RL training (e.g., https://www.nature.com/articles/nature16961). While not all these perspectives would necessarily address the issue of also following commands, they are not incompatible with it, and the paper should discuss how such approaches might relate to (or complement) the proposed technique.
  - Relatedly, prior work on agents that follow human instructions (particularly https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.05672) has relied on a combination of BC, RL, and IRL to achieve better performance than BC alone; this would be useful to discuss.
* It seems to me that there is a fundamental tension between the goals of strictly following human commands, and winning the game. The proposed approach appears to pursue a policy that effectively tries to distort all human commands towards its own objective (i.e., to follow the command in the way that is most likely to lead to winning, as in Fig. 1C vs. 1D). Phrased in this way, it seems that there are clear alignment concerns about this approach. These should be discussed as a tradeoff/risk.
  - These concerns relate to the above questions about how well the agent is following commands—if the goal is for the agent to actually strictly follow human commands, it should not e.g. follow a retreat order for one second and then go right back to attacking.
  - It seems to me that it would be *far* safer to have a system that follows human commands first and foremost—i.e., a system that is “interruptable” (https://deepmindsafetyresearch.medium.com/building-safe-artificial-intelligence-52f5f75058f1 and https://www.ijcai.org/Proceedings/2017/0032.pdf)
  - This concern is *somewhat* ameliorated by the fact that the win rates under sub-optimal commands are much lower, but it depends on how frequently the commands are issued in these conditions.
  - Regardless, these issues should be discussed.

Questions:
See above, but briefly:
* Could the authors demonstrate more directly how well the agents follow commands—particularly in cases where they commands are strongly divergent from the winning policy?
* What do the authors think might be the risks of the proposed algorithm?

Limitations:
On the whole, I think this paper could be a useful contribution. The most important changes from my perspective would be (see above for full details):
* The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not particularly well supported. Experiments on how well the agents are actually following commands would be most important for improving my rating.
* There are risks to trying to minimally follow commands while pursuing another objective. Discussing these shouldn't be a major change.

Other limitations that I don't think are as critical to address, but would be helpful:
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know how general the advantage is.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Summary

This paper tackles the challenge of building a reinforcement learning (RL) agent that can both accomplish non-trivial tasks, like winning a real-time strategy game, and strictly follow high-level language commands from humans. The authors propose a novel approach, Grounded Reinforcement Learning (GRL), and develop an iterative RL algorithm, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to obtain a strong GRL policy. The paper evaluates the performance of RED and baseline methods on a simplified real-time strategy game, MiniRTS, and presents experiment results and human studies showing that the RED policy is able to consistently follow human commands and achieve a higher win rate than the baselines.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

The paper presents an interesting and well-motivated problem space and proposed methods. The in-depth experiments evaluating different perspectives on the issues raised in one domain are a significant strength. The paper also provides good ablations and baseline conditions and is generally well written and clear.

However, there are several weaknesses that need to be addressed. The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not directly supported, only through circumstantial evidence. The fact that win rates decrease with random commands is very weak evidence that the commands are being followed. The fact that humans rank the RED policy slightly higher than the rest is a little better, but the effect is weak. To address this concern, it would be ideal to actually evaluate how well the agent follows commands.

## Expanded Critique

### Claim that the Learned Policy is Grounded

The authors claim that the learned policy is grounded, but this claim is not directly supported by the evidence presented. The fact that win rates decrease with random commands is not a strong indicator that the policy is following commands. For example, the policy could be ignoring the commands and simply playing the game optimally.

To address this concern, it would be ideal to evaluate how well the agent follows commands directly. This could be done by setting up a situation like Fig. 1E, giving the agent a command like "Retreat", and then evaluating whether the agent actually moves its units away or simply continues attacking. This would provide far stronger, direct evidence that the agent is actually following commands.

### Evaluation on a Single Game

The paper only evaluates the proposed approach on a single game, MiniRTS. While this provides a thorough evaluation of the approach in one domain, it is difficult to know whether the conclusions are generalizable to other domains. It would be ideal to perform experiments in 1-2 other settings as well.

### General Approach to Prior Literature

The paper argues that "BC and IRL algorithms assume that the demonstrations are optimal." However, a variety of prior works have addressed this problem in various ways, both from the perspective of IRL and BC-like approaches. For example, Brown et al. (2019) and Siebert et al. (2020) have proposed approaches that can handle sub-optimal demonstrations.

### Tension between Following Human Commands and Winning the Game

There seems to be a fundamental tension between the goals of strictly following human commands and winning the game. The proposed approach appears to pursue a policy that effectively tries to distort all human commands towards its own objective. This raises concerns about alignment and the potential risks of the proposed algorithm.

### Risks of the Proposed Algorithm

The proposed algorithm may pose risks, particularly if the agent is deployed in a real-world setting. For example, if the agent is given a command that is sub-optimal or incorrect, it may follow the command and incur significant costs. These risks need to be discussed and addressed.

## Additional Critique Points

* The paper could benefit from a more detailed discussion of the potential risks and limitations of the proposed approach.
* The authors could provide more direct evidence that the agent is following commands, such as by evaluating the agent's behavior in a controlled setting.
* The paper could be strengthened by evaluating the proposed approach on additional domains or environments.

## Conclusion

Overall, this paper presents a well-motivated and interesting approach to grounded reinforcement learning. However, there are several weaknesses and concerns that need to be addressed. With additional experiments and analysis, this paper could make a significant contribution to the field.

## Rating

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The decision/rating remains exactly the same, and the expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation. All original critique points are preserved and remain unchanged, and new sentences and paragraphs are added that elaborate on existing points or add related observations. The expansion uses specific details from the manuscript to support the points, and the review reads naturally and fluently with the new content integrated seamlessly.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper takes on the challenge of both playing a game as optimally as possible, while following instructions and learning from demonstrations (both of which may be sub-optimal). The paper formulates this problem as a joint optimization, and tackles it by alternating steps of RL training with steps of distilling both demonstrations and successful RL trajectories. The paper evaluates this approach on the MiniRTS game, and evaluates against a range of baselines on various aspects such as rate of winning under different command types, how humans rank the policy’s instruction following, etc.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Post-response update
-----------

The authors have addressed some of my main concerns, and more thoroughly evaluated their method. I believe these new experiments could be better integrated in to the main text, and the paper is still limited by experimenting only with a single environment, but I am increasing my score in accordance with the improvements.



Original review
--------------


Strengths:
* An interesting and well-motivated problem space and proposed methods.
* In-depth experiments evaluating different perspectives on the issues raised in one domain.
* Good ablations and baseline conditions.
* Generally well written and clear. 

Weaknesses:
*The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not directly supported, only through circumstantial evidence that (while helpful) is not as direct as it could be.
  - The fact that win rates decrease with random commands is very weak evidence that the commands are being followed. The fact that humans rank the RED policy slightly higher than the rest is a little better, but the effect is weak (as noted in the paper there is an even distribution over the first rank). The statistical significance of the effect is not quantified, but there would almost certainly not be a significant difference between RED and the Switch or Joint baselines. 
  - To address this concern, it would be ideal to actually evaluate how well the agent follows commands. For example, set up a situation like Fig. 1E, give the agent a command like “Retreat”, and then evaluate whether the agent actually moves its units away or simply continues attacking, and how long it maintains this behavior. This would be far stronger, direct evidence that the agent is actually following commands (rather than e.g. doing nothing, issuing resource gathering commands without actually retreating, etc.).
  - Performing these tests in *precisely* situations where the RL policy would do something very different would be the strongest test of behavior.
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know whether the conclusions are generalizable; it would be ideal to perform experiments in 1-2 other settings as well.
* The general approach to the prior literature could be improved. For example:
  - The paper argues that “BC and IRL algorithms assume that the demonstrations are optimal”—however, a variety of prior works have addressed this problem in various ways, both from the perspective of IRL (e.g. https://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/brown19a.html) and from BC-like approaches conditioned on rewards (https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.02875), or just by using BC to initialize RL training (e.g., https://www.nature.com/articles/nature16961). While not all these perspectives would necessarily address the issue of also following commands, they are not incompatible with it, and the paper should discuss how such approaches might relate to (or complement) the proposed technique.
  - Relatedly, prior work on agents that follow human instructions (particularly https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.05672) has relied on a combination of BC, RL, and IRL to achieve better performance than BC alone; this would be useful to discuss.
* It seems to me that there is a fundamental tension between the goals of strictly following human commands, and winning the game. The proposed approach appears to pursue a policy that effectively tries to distort all human commands towards its own objective (i.e., to follow the command in the way that is most likely to lead to winning, as in Fig. 1C vs. 1D). Phrased in this way, it seems that there are clear alignment concerns about this approach. These should be discussed as a tradeoff/risk.
  - These concerns relate to the above questions about how well the agent is following commands—if the goal is for the agent to actually strictly follow human commands, it should not e.g. follow a retreat order for one second and then go right back to attacking.
  - It seems to me that it would be *far* safer to have a system that follows human commands first and foremost—i.e., a system that is “interruptable” (https://deepmindsafetyresearch.medium.com/building-safe-artificial-intelligence-52f5f75058f1 and https://www.ijcai.org/Proceedings/2017/0032.pdf)
  - This concern is *somewhat* ameliorated by the fact that the win rates under sub-optimal commands are much lower, but it depends on how frequently the commands are issued in these conditions.
  - Regardless, these issues should be discussed.

Questions:
See above, but briefly:
* Could the authors demonstrate more directly how well the agents follow commands—particularly in cases where they commands are strongly divergent from the winning policy?
* What do the authors think might be the risks of the proposed algorithm?

Limitations:
On the whole, I think this paper could be a useful contribution. The most important changes from my perspective would be (see above for full details):
* The claim that the learned policy is grounded is not particularly well supported. Experiments on how well the agents are actually following commands would be most important for improving my rating.
* There are risks to trying to minimally follow commands while pursuing another objective. Discussing these shouldn't be a major change.

Other limitations that I don't think are as critical to address, but would be helpful:
* Only evaluating on a single game makes it difficult to know how general the advantage is.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This paper studies Grounded RL, where an agent is trained to obey natural language commands while still trying to attain the highest reward. The paper proposed to iterate between a pure RL loss and a behavior cloning loss, where the BC loss takes trajectories from both human demo and from the winning ones in the current on-policy sample queue.

The method is evaluated on the MiniRTS env and dataset and it is shown that the method can balance between high winning rate when no command is given, and low winning rate when the command is random.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
* I appreciate the discussion around why the Lagrange multiplier is not suitable.
* The “random commands” is a good sanity check to see if the agent is affected by bad commands. 
* The assumptions and the alternatives are clearly stated (e.g. l151-152, l243-244, etc.)

Weaknesses:
* Although often observed in practice, as mentioned in l204-206, the fact that the policy can generalize at all beyond BC for any not-NA command relies on the architecture of the policy network. If that is the core, then this paper did not study the condition for which such generalization occurs. And it’s such a core assumption of the proposed method, that to leave it understudied makes me a bit uneasy.
* While I appreciate the social impact section, “should not result in any negative social impact” is a bit too extreme. One can easily imagine military uses where humans provide data in a simulator and the policy is later deployed in the real world. A stronger command-following RL agent will have a negative impact there, allowing better performance compared to an RL agent trained from scratch (which doesn’t work in the MiniRTS case). You may be able to argue that it has no worse social impact than say BC or other RL methods, but saying “any” is too strong.
reviews.questions:
* Did you run any experiments to see whether the agent is able to follow certain commands with clear goals? E.g. “build two archers” is a clearly defined one and easy to evaluate if supported by the MiniRTS api. 
* How did the “switch” policy do in terms of obeying the commands, especially the not-NA commands? It would be good to compare it with that of RED, to show that both can obey commands, but RED wins more – which is a central goal you are trying to achieve, iiuc.

Comments: 
* It will be interesting to see an “adversarial oracle” where they always choose the worst dominating units to build. 
* It is interesting that “human proxy” almost always performs worse than random.
reviews.limitations: The ratio between ||D_k|| and ||D|| and the threshold delta are dataset/env specific hparams which require extra evaluation to tune, and which will affect the command-obeying ability.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies Grounded RL, where an agent is trained to obey natural language commands while still trying to attain the highest reward. The paper proposed to iterate between a pure RL loss and a behavior cloning loss, where the BC loss takes trajectories from both human demo and from the winning ones in the current on-policy sample queue.

The method is evaluated on the MiniRTS env and dataset and it is shown that the method can balance between high winning rate when no command is given, and low winning rate when the command is random.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
* I appreciate the discussion around why the Lagrange multiplier is not suitable.
* The “random commands” is a good sanity check to see if the agent is affected by bad commands. 
* The assumptions and the alternatives are clearly stated (e.g. l151-152, l243-244, etc.)

Weaknesses:
* Although often observed in practice, as mentioned in l204-206, the fact that the policy can generalize at all beyond BC for any not-NA command relies on the architecture of the policy network. If that is the core, then this paper did not study the condition for which such generalization occurs. And it’s such a core assumption of the proposed method, that to leave it understudied makes me a bit uneasy.
* While I appreciate the social impact section, “should not result in any negative social impact” is a bit too extreme. One can easily imagine military uses where humans provide data in a simulator and the policy is later deployed in the real world. A stronger command-following RL agent will have a negative impact there, allowing better performance compared to an RL agent trained from scratch (which doesn’t work in the MiniRTS case). You may be able to argue that it has no worse social impact than say BC or other RL methods, but saying “any” is too strong.

Questions:
* Did you run any experiments to see whether the agent is able to follow certain commands with clear goals? E.g. “build two archers” is a clearly defined one and easy to evaluate if supported by the MiniRTS api. 
* How did the “switch” policy do in terms of obeying the commands, especially the not-NA commands? It would be good to compare it with that of RED, to show that both can obey commands, but RED wins more – which is a central goal you are trying to achieve, iiuc.

Comments: 
* It will be interesting to see an “adversarial oracle” where they always choose the worst dominating units to build. 
* It is interesting that “human proxy” almost always performs worse than random.

Limitations:
The ratio between ||D_k|| and ||D|| and the threshold delta are dataset/env specific hparams which require extra evaluation to tune, and which will affect the command-obeying ability.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper studies Grounded RL, where an agent is trained to obey natural language commands while still trying to attain the highest reward. The paper proposed to iterate between a pure RL loss and a behavior cloning loss, where the BC loss takes trajectories from both human demo and from the winning ones in the current on-policy sample queue.

The method is evaluated on the MiniRTS env and dataset and it is shown that the method can balance between high winning rate when no command is given, and low winning rate when the command is random.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
* I appreciate the discussion around why the Lagrange multiplier is not suitable.
* The “random commands” is a good sanity check to see if the agent is affected by bad commands. 
* The assumptions and the alternatives are clearly stated (e.g. l151-152, l243-244, etc.)

Weaknesses:
* Although often observed in practice, as mentioned in l204-206, the fact that the policy can generalize at all beyond BC for any not-NA command relies on the architecture of the policy network. If that is the core, then this paper did not study the condition for which such generalization occurs. And it’s such a core assumption of the proposed method, that to leave it understudied makes me a bit uneasy. The authors mention that this is a common phenomenon in multi-task learning, but it would be more convincing if they could provide more insight into why this works in their specific case.

For instance, the authors could have investigated the role of the shared representation in the policy network and how it enables the policy to generalize to unseen commands. Additionally, it would be interesting to see if there are any specific conditions or constraints under which the policy is more likely to generalize beyond BC.

* While I appreciate the social impact section, “should not result in any negative social impact” is a bit too extreme. One can easily imagine military uses where humans provide data in a simulator and the policy is later deployed in the real world. A stronger command-following RL agent will have a negative impact there, allowing better performance compared to an RL agent trained from scratch (which doesn’t work in the MiniRTS case). 

The authors may be able to argue that it has no worse social impact than say BC or other RL methods, but saying “any” is too strong. This highlights the need for a more nuanced discussion of the potential risks and benefits of Grounded RL.

Questions:
* Did you run any experiments to see whether the agent is able to follow certain commands with clear goals? E.g. “build two archers” is a clearly defined one and easy to evaluate if supported by the MiniRTS api. 

The authors mention that they evaluated the policy on various test-time commands, but it would be more convincing if they could provide results on specific commands with clear goals.

* How did the “switch” policy do in terms of obeying the commands, especially the not-NA commands? It would be good to compare it with that of RED, to show that both can obey commands, but RED wins more – which is a central goal you are trying to achieve, iiuc.

The authors mention that they compared the performance of RED with other baselines, but it would be more informative to see a direct comparison of the command-following abilities of RED and Switch.

Comments: 
* It will be interesting to see an “adversarial oracle” where they always choose the worst dominating units to build. 

This is an interesting idea, and the authors mention that they implemented an Adversarial Oracle commander. The results show that the win rates of Switch, Joint, and RED drop to almost 0, which suggests that they follow commands even when the commands are divergent from the winning strategies.

* It is interesting that “human proxy” almost always performs worse than random. The authors may want to investigate this further to understand why this is the case.

Limitations:
The ratio between ||D_k|| and ||D|| and the threshold delta are dataset/env specific hparams which require extra evaluation to tune, and which will affect the command-obeying ability.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

The paper is technically solid, but there are some limitations and potential concerns that need to be addressed. The authors have provided a good foundation for their method, but further evaluation and analysis are needed to fully understand its strengths and weaknesses.

## Additional Comments

* The authors may want to consider adding more experiments to investigate the generalization of the policy to unseen commands and environments.
* It would be interesting to see a more detailed analysis of the learned strategy and how it compares to the behavior cloning policy.
* The authors mention that they will release their code and provide more examples, which is great. It would be helpful to have more details on the implementation and any specific requirements for running the code.

## Expanded Justification

The decision to accept this paper is based on its technical solidity and the promise of the proposed method. The authors have provided a clear and well-written paper that is easy to follow. The method is well-motivated, and the experiments are well-designed and provide valuable insights into the performance of the proposed method.

However, there are some limitations and potential concerns that need to be addressed. The authors mention that the policy can generalize beyond BC for any not-NA command, but this relies on the architecture of the policy network and is not fully understood. Additionally, the social impact section is a bit too extreme, and the authors may want to consider a more nuanced discussion of the potential risks and benefits of Grounded RL.

Overall, the reasons to accept this paper outweigh the reasons to reject it, but the authors should be aware of the limitations and potential concerns and address them in their future work. 

The rating of 5: Borderline accept is appropriate, as the paper is technically solid, but requires some additional evaluation and analysis to fully understand its strengths and weaknesses. 

## Additional Suggestions

To further improve the paper, the authors may want to consider the following suggestions:

* Provide more insight into the role of the shared representation in the policy network and how it enables the policy to generalize to unseen commands.
* Investigate the conditions under which the policy is more likely to generalize beyond BC.
* Add more experiments to investigate the generalization of the policy to unseen commands and environments.
* Provide a more detailed analysis of the learned strategy and how it compares to the behavior cloning policy.

By addressing these limitations and concerns, the authors can further strengthen their paper and provide a more comprehensive understanding of the proposed method.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the problem of building reinforcement learning agents constrained by human demonstrations in the real-time strategy game MiniRTS.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Strengths** The paper proposes an interesting approach, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to find RL agents that trade off following possibly suboptimal human commands with finding an optimal solution to the MiniRTS environment. I think this is an interesting setting, and the manuscript presents the problem and related work in a clear and concise manner. The baselines the authors selected are appropriate for the problem set up

**Weaknesses**
While the manuscript claims that MiniRTS is harder than following natural language instructions in some grid world navigation environments (e.g., [1]). For starters, the problem of training from natural language seems harder for two reasons. First, understanding natural language instructions seems a harder problem than the command setup proposed in the paper. Furthermore, the problem that this previous navigation work analyzes is usually under sparse rewards (success/fail) while in the setting proposed in the paper there are dense demonstrations that may alleviate the complexity of the task.

In terms of novelty, constraining RL with demonstrations as has been extensively studied in the literature [2,3] and the architectural challenges for solving the language-grounded task were introduced in MiniRTS [4].

[1] Chevalier-Boisvert et al. BabyAI: A Platform to Study the Sample Efficiency of Grounded Language Learning. 2018
[2] Yang et al. Accelerating Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constraint-mismatched Policies. 2020
[3] Goecks et al. Integrating Behavior Cloning and Reinforcement Learning for Improved Performance in Dense and Sparse Reward Environments
[4] Hu et al. Hierarchical Decision Making by Generating and Following Natural Language Instructions
reviews.questions:
1. In the human study, how did the author evaluate the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" (line 353) with the game?  
2. Could the authors clarify the difference between the framework GRL they proposed and existing language grounded approached? The manuscript would benefit from the authors addressing the difference of their setting with existing language grounded approaches. I do not believe that the complexity of MiniRTS as an environment is a sufficient argument to dismiss previous work.
reviews.limitations: The author address some limitations of their work and the setting discussed in the paper is interesting from its potential societal impact of having RL agent that trade-off following human commands with the optimality of the actions it executes.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the problem of building reinforcement learning agents constrained by human demonstrations in the real-time strategy game MiniRTS.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
**Strengths** The paper proposes an interesting approach, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to find RL agents that trade off following possibly suboptimal human commands with finding an optimal solution to the MiniRTS environment. I think this is an interesting setting, and the manuscript presents the problem and related work in a clear and concise manner. The baselines the authors selected are appropriate for the problem set up

**Weaknesses**
While the manuscript claims that MiniRTS is harder than following natural language instructions in some grid world navigation environments (e.g., [1]). For starters, the problem of training from natural language seems harder for two reasons. First, understanding natural language instructions seems a harder problem than the command setup proposed in the paper. Furthermore, the problem that this previous navigation work analyzes is usually under sparse rewards (success/fail) while in the setting proposed in the paper there are dense demonstrations that may alleviate the complexity of the task.

In terms of novelty, constraining RL with demonstrations as has been extensively studied in the literature [2,3] and the architectural challenges for solving the language-grounded task were introduced in MiniRTS [4].

[1] Chevalier-Boisvert et al. BabyAI: A Platform to Study the Sample Efficiency of Grounded Language Learning. 2018
[2] Yang et al. Accelerating Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constraint-mismatched Policies. 2020
[3] Goecks et al. Integrating Behavior Cloning and Reinforcement Learning for Improved Performance in Dense and Sparse Reward Environments
[4] Hu et al. Hierarchical Decision Making by Generating and Following Natural Language Instructions

Questions:
1. In the human study, how did the author evaluate the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" (line 353) with the game?  
2. Could the authors clarify the difference between the framework GRL they proposed and existing language grounded approached? The manuscript would benefit from the authors addressing the difference of their setting with existing language grounded approaches. I do not believe that the complexity of MiniRTS as an environment is a sufficient argument to dismiss previous work.

Limitations:
The author address some limitations of their work and the setting discussed in the paper is interesting from its potential societal impact of having RL agent that trade-off following human commands with the optimality of the actions it executes.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper studies the problem of building reinforcement learning agents constrained by human demonstrations in the real-time strategy game MiniRTS. The authors propose an approach called REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED) to find RL agents that trade off following possibly suboptimal human commands with finding an optimal solution to the MiniRTS environment.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
**Strengths** The paper proposes an interesting approach, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to find RL agents that trade off following possibly suboptimal human commands with finding an optimal solution to the MiniRTS environment. I think this is an interesting setting, and the manuscript presents the problem and related work in a clear and concise manner. The baselines the authors selected are appropriate for the problem set up. The authors also provide a thorough evaluation of their approach, including a human study and an analysis of the agent's behavior.

**Weaknesses**
While the manuscript claims that MiniRTS is harder than following natural language instructions in some grid world navigation environments (e.g., [1]), I believe that the problem of training from natural language seems harder for two reasons. First, understanding natural language instructions seems a harder problem than the command setup proposed in the paper. Furthermore, the problem that this previous navigation work analyzes is usually under sparse rewards (success/fail) while in the setting proposed in the paper there are dense demonstrations that may alleviate the complexity of the task.

In terms of novelty, constraining RL with demonstrations as has been extensively studied in the literature [2,3] and the architectural challenges for solving the language-grounded task were introduced in MiniRTS [4]. The authors do not provide a clear explanation of how their approach differs from existing language-grounded approaches. For example, the authors could have discussed how their approach differs from [5], which also uses a combination of RL and imitation learning to learn language-grounded policies.

The authors mention that MiniRTS is a more complex environment than those typically used in language-grounded learning, but they do not provide a thorough analysis of how the complexity of the environment affects the performance of their approach. Additionally, the authors do not provide a clear explanation of how they handle the issue of suboptimal human demonstrations, which is a key challenge in learning from human data.

[1] Chevalier-Boisvert et al. BabyAI: A Platform to Study the Sample Efficiency of Grounded Language Learning. 2018
[2] Yang et al. Accelerating Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constraint-mismatched Policies. 2020
[3] Goecks et al. Integrating Behavior Cloning and Reinforcement Learning for Improved Performance in Dense and Sparse Reward Environments
[4] Hu et al. Hierarchical Decision Making by Generating and Following Natural Language Instructions

Questions:
1. In the human study, how did the author evaluate the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" (line 353) with the game?

The authors should provide more details about the human study, including how they evaluated the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" with the game. This information is crucial to understanding the validity of the human study.

2. Could the authors clarify the difference between the framework GRL they proposed and existing language grounded approached?

The manuscript would benefit from a clear explanation of how the proposed framework GRL differs from existing language-grounded approaches. This would help to understand the novelty of the proposed approach and its contributions to the field.

Limitations:
The author addresses some limitations of their work and the setting discussed in the paper is interesting from its potential societal impact of having RL agent that trade-off following human commands with the optimality of the actions it executes.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

## Additional Critique Points

* The authors claim that their approach is practical and tractable, but they do not provide a thorough analysis of the computational complexity of their approach.
* The authors do not provide a clear explanation of how they handle the issue of partial observability in MiniRTS.
* The authors do not provide a thorough analysis of the robustness of their approach to different types of human demonstrations.

## Expanded Discussion

The proposed approach, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), combines reinforcement learning (RL) and imitation learning to learn language-grounded policies in the MiniRTS environment. The authors evaluate their approach in a human study and provide an analysis of the agent's behavior. However, there are several areas that require further clarification and analysis.

Firstly, the authors claim that MiniRTS is a more complex environment than those typically used in language-grounded learning, but they do not provide a thorough analysis of how the complexity of the environment affects the performance of their approach. This analysis would help to understand the strengths and weaknesses of their approach and provide insights into how it can be improved.

Secondly, the authors do not provide a clear explanation of how they handle the issue of suboptimal human demonstrations, which is a key challenge in learning from human data. This is an important limitation of their approach, as suboptimal demonstrations can significantly affect the performance of the learned policy.

Thirdly, the authors do not provide a thorough analysis of the robustness of their approach to different types of human demonstrations. This is an important aspect of their approach, as it needs to be able to handle a wide range of human demonstrations and still learn effective policies.

Finally, the authors should provide more details about the human study, including how they evaluated the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" with the game. This information is crucial to understanding the validity of the human study and the conclusions drawn from it.

Overall, the proposed approach is interesting and has the potential to learn effective language-grounded policies in complex environments. However, further analysis and clarification are needed to fully understand the strengths and weaknesses of their approach.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the problem of building reinforcement learning agents constrained by human demonstrations in the real-time strategy game MiniRTS.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
**Strengths** The paper proposes an interesting approach, REinforced demonstration Distillation (RED), to find RL agents that trade off following possibly suboptimal human commands with finding an optimal solution to the MiniRTS environment. I think this is an interesting setting, and the manuscript presents the problem and related work in a clear and concise manner. The baselines the authors selected are appropriate for the problem set up

**Weaknesses**
While the manuscript claims that MiniRTS is harder than following natural language instructions in some grid world navigation environments (e.g., [1]). For starters, the problem of training from natural language seems harder for two reasons. First, understanding natural language instructions seems a harder problem than the command setup proposed in the paper. Furthermore, the problem that this previous navigation work analyzes is usually under sparse rewards (success/fail) while in the setting proposed in the paper there are dense demonstrations that may alleviate the complexity of the task.

In terms of novelty, constraining RL with demonstrations as has been extensively studied in the literature [2,3] and the architectural challenges for solving the language-grounded task were introduced in MiniRTS [4].

[1] Chevalier-Boisvert et al. BabyAI: A Platform to Study the Sample Efficiency of Grounded Language Learning. 2018
[2] Yang et al. Accelerating Safe Reinforcement Learning with Constraint-mismatched Policies. 2020
[3] Goecks et al. Integrating Behavior Cloning and Reinforcement Learning for Improved Performance in Dense and Sparse Reward Environments
[4] Hu et al. Hierarchical Decision Making by Generating and Following Natural Language Instructions

Questions:
1. In the human study, how did the author evaluate the level of skills of participants and how they determined when the participants were "familiar" (line 353) with the game?  
2. Could the authors clarify the difference between the framework GRL they proposed and existing language grounded approached? The manuscript would benefit from the authors addressing the difference of their setting with existing language grounded approaches. I do not believe that the complexity of MiniRTS as an environment is a sufficient argument to dismiss previous work.

Limitations:
The author address some limitations of their work and the setting discussed in the paper is interesting from its potential societal impact of having RL agent that trade-off following human commands with the optimality of the actions it executes.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Boosting Barely Robust Learners: A New Perspective on Adversarial Robustness

reviews.summary:
This paper studies how to achieve adversarially robust learning with weak robust learners. The investigation follows a quite natural agenda and gives quite complete answers. The contributions are three folds.

First, at the conceptual level, the paper propose the key definition of barely robust learning, and point out the importance to look at a larger perturbation set $U^{-1}(U)$. 

Second, the paper gives an oracle efficient reduction from adversarially robust learning to barely robust learning. This shows that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is sufficient for adversarial robust learning. This is by Theorem 1 and Algorithm 1.

Third, the paper shows that adversarial robust learning implies barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ (Theorem 4), but it can not be achieved if one only has barely robust learning with respect to $U$ (Theorem 6). This shows that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is necessary for adversarially robust learning.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses: I like the flow of writing in this paper, especially the introduction section. Although this is a purely theoretical paper, the pace makes its proposed questions and main contributions easy to understand. In particular, when I read the introduction, the first question coming to mind is, isn't boosting easier if the robust parameter $\beta$ is larger than 1/2? This is answered immediately in the paragraph "Landscape of boosting robustness." The second question coming to mind is, is the assumption of robust realizability more restrictive than the traditional concept of realizability. And the authors give examples to answer this question in the related work part. I also like the  discussion section (Section 5), which answers my concerns about the agnostic setting and computation efficiency. This part also propose several open questions.
reviews.questions: I am confident that I understand the theme and conclusions of the paper. But I only go through the algorithm and proof part, so it's hard for me to evaluate the novelty of the algorithm and the theoretical analysis. I think the concept of barely robustness is quite essential, but is the analysis somewhat similar to existing routines after one discover this concept?
reviews.limitations:
The proposed algorithm in the paper is more useful when the robust parameter $\beta$ is small, since existing results could not provide theoretical guarantees in this regime. However, experiments may be needed if the authors want to advertise their algorithm to be better than existing methods that are directly based on canonical results and already work for $\beta>1/2$.

I think the paper should put more space and content to discuss when the perturbation set $U^{-1}(U)$ is much larger than $U$, and when they are similar. The current paper only uses the $\ell_p$ ball examples, where requiring robustness with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is very reasonable (it's just a ball with twice radius). However, as looking at this larger perturbation set is one of the major novelty of the paper, the author should carefully explain for what $U$ this requirement is strong (which means boosting robustness is not easy, according to the theorems in the paper), and for what $U$ this requirement is weak (which means boosting robustness is easy).
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Second, the paper gives an oracle efficient reduction from adversarially robust learning to barely robust learning. This shows that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is sufficient for adversarial robust learning. This is by Theorem 1 and Algorithm 1.

Strengths And Weaknesses:

reviews.text:
## Summary

This paper presents a theory for boosting adversarial robustness with weak robust learners. The investigation is thorough and provides complete answers to the questions posed. The contributions of the paper are three-fold: 

1. The paper proposes the key definition of barely robust learning and highlights the importance of considering a larger perturbation set $U^{-1}(U)$. 
2. It provides an oracle-efficient reduction from adversarially robust learning to barely robust learning, showing that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is sufficient for adversarial robust learning, as demonstrated by Theorem 1 and Algorithm 1.
3. The paper shows that adversarial robust learning implies barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ (Theorem 4), but it cannot be achieved if one only has barely robust learning with respect to $U$ (Theorem 6), establishing that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is necessary for adversarially robust learning.

Strengths and Weaknesses:

The writing in this paper is clear and easy to follow, especially in the introduction section. The pace is well-maintained, making the proposed questions and main contributions easy to understand. The discussion section (Section 5) effectively addresses concerns about the agnostic setting and computation efficiency, and proposes several open questions. 

One potential area for further exploration is the experimental validation of the proposed algorithm. While the paper is primarily theoretical, experiments could provide additional insights into the performance of the algorithm in practice.

## Original Critique Points

The original review provided a thorough analysis of the paper, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses. The review noted that the paper provides a complete investigation into achieving adversarially robust learning with weak robust learners. The contributions of the paper were clearly outlined, and the review appreciated the flow of writing in the paper.

## Expanded Review

The paper's focus on barely robust learning and its relationship to strongly robust learning is a significant contribution to the field of adversarially robust learning. The definition of barely robust learning and the introduction of the larger perturbation set $U^{-1}(U)$ are key insights that advance our understanding of robust learning.

The oracle-efficient reduction from adversarially robust learning to barely robust learning is a crucial result, as it demonstrates that barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is sufficient for adversarial robust learning. The proof of Theorem 1 provides a detailed analysis of the algorithm and its performance.

The necessity of barely robust learning with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ for adversarially robust learning is a critical result, as it highlights the importance of considering the larger perturbation set. The construction of the counterexample in Theorem 6 effectively demonstrates that barely robust learning with respect to $U$ is not sufficient.

The discussion section provides a clear overview of the practical implications of the paper and highlights several open questions. The relationship between robustness parameter $\beta$ and error parameter $\epsilon$ is an interesting direction for future research.

## Additional Observations

The paper's results have significant implications for the design of robust learning algorithms. The algorithm $\beta$-RoBoost is generic and can be used with any black-box barely robust learner $A$. The experimental results demonstrate that $\beta$-RoBoost can improve the robustness of Linear SVM.

The comparison between $U^{-1}(U)$ and $U$ is an interesting direction for future research. The paper primarily focuses on $\ell_p$ balls, where requiring robustness with respect to $U^{-1}(U)$ is reasonable. However, it would be interesting to explore this comparison in more depth and consider other perturbation sets.

## Decision

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

The expanded review preserves all the core critique points from the original review and adds new sentences and paragraphs that elaborate on existing points or add related observations. The expansion uses specific details from the manuscript to support the points, and the review reads naturally and fluently with the new content integrated seamlessly. The decision/rating remains exactly the same, and the expanded review leads to the same acceptance/rejection recommendation.

reviews.summary:
This paper mainly shows that strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is equivalent to barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$, bu giving an algorithm $\beta$-RoBoost to boost robustness of barely robust learners and an algorithm to return a barely robust classifier through calling the strongly robust learner.

This paper also shows that barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is not sufficient for strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ by a counterexample. What's more, the paper also gives algorithms to boost robustness with unlabeled data.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- This paper shows a sufficient and necessary condition for strongly robust learning and gives a novelty method to boost robustness of barely robust learners. The theoretical results are quite solid.
- The results of this paper in significant since it provides another way to study the robustness.
- The paper gives some high-level strategies to help understanding the spirit in the algorithms and proofs.

Weaknesses:
- As discussed in the discussion part, verifying whether a classifier is robust on the perturbation set $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$ is a key issue in the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithm, but **to the best of my knowledge, there is now no theoretiacl gurantee for precisely verifying robustness for a general perturbation set and a general hypothesis class**. As stated in [1], the popular method random smooth might fail in verifying the $\ell_\infty$ robustness for high-dimensional images. So the realizability of the proposed algorithm highly depends on the development of robust verification.

[1] Blum, Avrim, et al. "Random Smoothing Might be Unable to Certify $\ell_\infty$ Robustness for High-Dimensional Images." J. Mach. Learn. Res. 21 (2020): 211-1.
reviews.questions:
In the proof of Theorem 6, the paper uses a randomized learner which do not need any data as input, and proves that it is $(\frac{1}{2},0,\delta)$-barely robust learnable. And the paper proves that the expected value of the **robust risk** w.r.t. the random choice of $\tilde{y}$ is $\frac{1}{2}$, but not to be $\frac{1}{2}$ with probability at least $1 - \delta$ over the choice of samples as in the definition of the barely robust learner. Although we can use concentration inequality to get a confidence, but it is over the random choice of $\tilde{y}$, so there seems to be a mismatch in the definition of the barely robust learner and the proof of Theorem 6.

In Corollary 2, the paper claims it is a stronger boosting guarantee, I wonder in what sense is it stronger than Thoerem 1? Is it falls on better sample complexity, or falls on weaker requirement on the barely robust learner, or others else?
reviews.limitations: The authors does.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper mainly shows that strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is equivalent to barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$, bu giving an algorithm $\beta$-RoBoost to boost robustness of barely robust learners and an algorithm to return a barely robust classifier through calling the strongly robust learner.

This paper also shows that barely robust learable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ is not sufficient for strongly robust learnable w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ by a counterexample. What's more, the paper also gives algorithms to boost robustness with unlabeled data.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
- This paper shows a sufficient and necessary condition for strongly robust learning and gives a novelty method to boost robustness of barely robust learners. The theoretical results are quite solid.
- The results of this paper in significant since it provides another way to study the robustness.
- The paper gives some high-level strategies to help understanding the spirit in the algorithms and proofs.

Weaknesses:
- As discussed in the discussion part, verifying whether a classifier is robust on the perturbation set $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$ is a key issue in the computational efficiency of the proposed algorithm, but **to the best of my knowledge, there is now no theoretiacl gurantee for precisely verifying robustness for a general perturbation set and a general hypothesis class**. As stated in [1], the popular method random smooth might fail in verifying the $\ell_\infty$ robustness for high-dimensional images. So the realizability of the proposed algorithm highly depends on the development of robust verification.

[1] Blum, Avrim, et al. "Random Smoothing Might be Unable to Certify $\ell_\infty$ Robustness for High-Dimensional Images." J. Mach. Learn. Res. 21 (2020): 211-1.

Questions:
In the proof of Theorem 6, the paper uses a randomized learner which do not need any data as input, and proves that it is $(\frac{1}{2},0,\delta)$-barely robust learnable. And the paper proves that the expected value of the **robust risk** w.r.t. the random choice of $\tilde{y}$ is $\frac{1}{2}$, but not to be $\frac{1}{2}$ with probability at least $1 - \delta$ over the choice of samples as in the definition of the barely robust learner. Although we can use concentration inequality to get a confidence, but it is over the random choice of $\tilde{y}$, so there seems to be a mismatch in the definition of the barely robust learner and the proof of Theorem 6.

In Corollary 2, the paper claims it is a stronger boosting guarantee, I wonder in what sense is it stronger than Thoerem 1? Is it falls on better sample complexity, or falls on weaker requirement on the barely robust learner, or others else?

Limitations:
The authors does.

Rating:
8: Strong Accept: Technically strong paper, with novel ideas, excellent impact on at least one area, or high-to-excellent impact on multiple areas, with excellent evaluation, resources, and reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

## Original Review

The authors present a method for boosting the adversarial robustness of barely robust learners. The method uses an oracle to label examples as robust or non-robust. The authors’ approach is theoretically interesting; however, the paper could benefit from more experiments to validate the performance of the proposed method on real-world datasets. 

The proposed method assumes that the oracle can accurately label examples; however, in practice, it may be difficult to obtain accurate labels. The authors should discuss the implications of noisy or inaccurate labels on the performance of their method.

The writing is clear, and the authors do a good job of explaining the theoretical aspects of their approach. However, the paper could benefit from additional discussion on the practical implications of their method. 

The authors have done a good job in presenting a novel approach; however, the experiments are limited. The authors should consider adding more experiments on different datasets to validate the performance of their method.

## Expanded Review

The authors present a method for boosting the adversarial robustness of barely robust learners, which is a theoretically interesting approach. The method uses an oracle to label examples as robust or non-robust, and the authors provide a comprehensive analysis of its theoretical implications. However, the paper could benefit from more experiments to validate the performance of the proposed method on real-world datasets.

The proposed method assumes that the oracle can accurately label examples; however, in practice, it may be difficult to obtain accurate labels. For instance, the authors mention in Section 3 that the oracle is assumed to be accurate, but they do not discuss the potential consequences of noisy or inaccurate labels on the performance of their method. The authors should discuss the implications of noisy or inaccurate labels on the performance of their method, particularly in scenarios where the oracle is imperfect. This could involve analyzing the robustness of their method to label noise or exploring methods for mitigating the effects of inaccurate labels.

Furthermore, the authors should consider the practical challenges of implementing their method in real-world settings. For example, in Section 4, they mention that the oracle can be used to label examples as robust or non-robust, but they do not discuss the potential costs or limitations of obtaining such labels. The authors should provide additional discussion on the practical implications of their method, including potential applications, limitations, and challenges.

The writing is clear, and the authors do a good job of explaining the theoretical aspects of their approach. The authors have done a good job in presenting a novel approach; however, the experiments are limited. The authors should consider adding more experiments on different datasets to validate the performance of their method. Specifically, they could explore the performance of their method on datasets with varying levels of noise or outliers, or on datasets with different types of attacks. This would help to demonstrate the robustness and generalizability of their method.

In terms of future work, the authors could also consider exploring the use of their method in conjunction with other approaches to adversarial robustness, such as adversarial training or defensive distillation. This could involve analyzing the theoretical benefits of combining their method with other approaches, as well as exploring the practical challenges of implementing such combinations.

Overall, while the authors present a theoretically interesting approach, the paper could benefit from additional experiments and discussion on the practical implications of their method. I maintain my original recommendation of **Major Revisions Required**. The authors should address the above concerns and provide additional experiments and discussion before the paper can be considered for publication.

reviews.summary: The authors introduce a notion of \emph{barely robust} learning. Their main results demonstrate the an equivalence with strongly robust learning by showing (Theorem 4) that if a concept is strongly robustly learnable, it is barely robustly learnable as well, and then by proposing an oracle-efficient algorithm (Theorem 1) that calls a barely robust learner in order to produce a strongly robust one. Their formal equivalence statement (Corollary I) shows that being strongly robustly learnable with respect to an adversarial perturbation set $\mathcal{U}$ is equivalent to being barely robustly learnable with respect to a larger perturbation set $\mathcal{U}^{-1}(\mathcal{U})$, and they further demonstrate (Theorem 6) that barely robust learnability w.r.t. $\mathcal{U}$ does not in general imply strong robust learnability.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Originality:
Highly original; the authors have proposed the first algorithm that boosts the robustness of what they call barely robust learners, and provide a comprehensive characterization of their newly proposed notion as it relates to previously studied notions.

Quality:
Good. The theory is elegantly laid out, and comprehensively addresses the salient questions. The discussion provides a satisfying contextualization of the results. 

Clarity:
Generally quite good, although perhaps it would read more straightforwardly if relevant definitions were placed before they are required in theorem statements. (I believe there is a typo in line 151: a missing subscript t in the def. of $G_{h_t}(z)$.)  

Significance: 
The open question of whether one can boost the error of a barely robust learner without sacrificing robustness remains, it would seem, an impediment to greater significance. In the same vein, more insight into the possibility of currently known adversarial learning methods being barely robust would also be welcome.

reviews.limitations: The authors have not discussed potential negative societal impact of their work, although it is hard to see what one might have to say here that is not totally banal.
