title: DEQGAN: Learning the Loss Function for PINNs with Generative Adversarial Networks

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a method to solve ordinary and partial differential equations using GANs. Following in the tradition of physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), the flow equation is parametrized by a neural network. The parameters of the neural network are optimised by replacing a hand-tuned loss function such as the mean-squared error, with a discriminator and an adversarial loss. This method appears to provide improved performance over PINNs trained with hand-tuned loss functions and numerical solvers, on several well-known differential equations.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1) The paper is very well-written, well-structured and easy to follow. The method and results, for the most part, are described in clear detail.
2) The ablation study, and discussion of methods to ensure stable training were extremely interesting and relevant, given that GANs are known to be difficult to train.
2) The extensive results in the paper make a convincing case for using GANs over other methods for solving differential equations -- in particular, with respect to the variability of the solutions from other methods across different problems.

Weaknesses:
1) Many of the details of training, hyperparameters etc., included in the methods section (sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4), while important for reproducibility, distract from the main message of the paper, and hinder readability.
2) Some of the results require a lot more elucidation: e.g. the details of the ablation study in section 5.2 are mostly relegated to the appendix, and thus make the import of the results hard to parse; the plots in Figure 3 are barely mentioned or described in the text. 
3) The paper could improve with a more extensive discussion of the limitations of the method (the conclusion mentions non-interpretability of the discriminator, but not much more).
4) The paper claims to present two "methods" to improve robustness in training: residual monitoring and addition of instance noise. While it is important to document the techniques used to stabilised training, neither of these are nescessarily novel contributions in the context of GANs. Furthermore, it is not clear whether these measures would be equally effective without spectral normalisation, skip connections in the generator, or with different choice of variance for the instance noise, or a change in the 25% training iteration threshold for residual monitoring.
reviews.questions:
As outlined in the previous section, I believe the paper makes a strong case for using GANs to solve differential equations. Some concrete suggestions to address the weaknesses:
1) It would be good to move the details of sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2 and 3.4 to the appendix, and move details of the ablation study to the main paper. Furthermore, sections 3.3 and 4.1 describe training / engineering choices to a level of detail that hinders readability, and can certainly be condensed.
2) It would be good to have a more extensive discussion of Figure 3. Moreover, Figure 3 appears to show only simulations from the GAN -- it would be good to have some examples in of the simulations from other methods as well, in order to contextualize the GAN performance beyond the reported MSE numbers.
3) While the methods residual monitoring and instance noise are certainly important details for reproducibility, it is not convincing that they would work without the other hyperparameter or architectural choices. It would therefore be good to modify the discussion of these two "methods", and to present them as potential avenues for stable training rather than a contribution of the paper.

Questions:
1) The performance of the different methods for solving differential equations are compared solely on the basis of MSE. Is there a particular reason for choosing this metric? -- it is particularly surprising that there is no justification provided for this in the paper, since the introduction makes a strong point that the choice of similarity metric typically used to optimize parameters for the PINNs are arbitrary. It would also be good to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics e.g. L1, or MMD 
2) In line 181-182, "We use the ReLU function...noise should not be used." -- this logic is not clear. Why should $L_g < L_d$ on any particular iteration indicate a better generator? -- this could also be due to initialisation, especially at the beginning of training.
3) How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

Minor comments:
1) The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in line 179-181 $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2) In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel
3) In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are note identified in the legend; the colourbars have no label in (c) and (d)
reviews.limitations:
It would be good if the discussion could address the following issues:
1) While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2) While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be good to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency and training times compared to the other methods.
3) How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:

Is there a particular reason for choosing this metric? -- it is particularly surprising that there is no justification provided for this in the paper, since the introduction makes a strong point that the choice of similarity metric typically used to optimize parameters for the PINNs are arbitrary. It would also be good to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics e.g. L1, or MMD 
2) In line 181-182, "We use the ReLU function...noise should not be used." -- this logic is not clear. Why should $L_g < L_d$ on any particular iteration indicate a better generator? -- this could also be due to initialisation, especially at the beginning of training.
3) How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

Minor comments:
1) The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in line 179-181 $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2) In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel
3) In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are note identified in the legend; the colourbars have no label in (c) and (d)

Limitations:
It would be good if the discussion could address the following issues:
1) While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2) While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be good to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency and training times compared to the other methods.
3) How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

### Summary

The paper proposes a method to solve ordinary and partial differential equations using Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). Following the tradition of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), the flow equation is parametrized by a neural network. The parameters of the neural network are optimized by replacing a hand-tuned loss function, such as the mean-squared error, with a discriminator and an adversarial loss. This method appears to provide improved performance over PINNs trained with hand-tuned loss functions and numerical solvers on several well-known differential equations.

### Strengths and Weaknesses

#### Strengths

1. The paper is very well-written, well-structured, and easy to follow. The method and results, for the most part, are described in clear detail.
2. The ablation study and discussion of methods to ensure stable training were extremely interesting and relevant, given that GANs are known to be difficult to train.
3. The extensive results in the paper make a convincing case for using GANs over other methods for solving differential equations, particularly with respect to the variability of the solutions from other methods across different problems.

#### Weaknesses

1. Many details of training, hyperparameters, etc., included in the methods section (sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, 3.4), while important for reproducibility, distract from the main message of the paper and hinder readability.
2. Some results require more elucidation, e.g., the details of the ablation study in section 5.2 are mostly relegated to the appendix, making the import of the results hard to parse; the plots in Figure 3 are barely mentioned or described in the text.
3. The paper could improve with a more extensive discussion of the limitations of the method (the conclusion mentions non-interpretability of the discriminator but not much more).
4. The paper claims to present two "methods" to improve robustness in training: residual monitoring and addition of instance noise. While it is important to document the techniques used to stabilize training, neither of these is necessarily a novel contribution in the context of GANs. Furthermore, it is not clear whether these measures would be equally effective without spectral normalization, skip connections in the generator, or with a different choice of variance for the instance noise, or a change in the 25% training iteration threshold for residual monitoring.

### Questions and Suggestions

1. **Reorganizing Details**: It would be beneficial to move the details of sections 3.2.1, 3.2.2, and 3.4 to the appendix and relocate details of the ablation study to the main paper. Furthermore, sections 3.3 and 4.1 describe training and engineering choices to a level of detail that hinders readability and can certainly be condensed.
2. **Discussing Figure 3**: A more extensive discussion of Figure 3 is warranted. Moreover, Figure 3 appears to show only simulations from the GAN; it would be beneficial to include examples from other methods as well to contextualize the GAN performance beyond the reported MSE numbers.
3. **Clarifying Methods**: While the methods of residual monitoring and instance noise are important details for reproducibility, it is not convincing that they would work without the other hyperparameter or architectural choices. It would be beneficial to modify the discussion of these two "methods" and present them as potential avenues for stable training rather than a contribution of the paper.

### Additional Comments

1. **Performance Metric**: The performance of the different methods for solving differential equations is compared solely on the basis of Mean Squared Error (MSE). Is there a particular reason for choosing this metric? It is particularly surprising that there is no justification provided for this in the paper, given that the introduction makes a strong point that the choice of similarity metric typically used to optimize parameters for PINNs is arbitrary. It would also be beneficial to see whether the GAN performance is equally good under other similarity metrics, e.g., L1 or MMD.
2. **Logic of Noise Addition**: In lines 181-182, "We use the ReLU function...noise should not be used." The logic is not clear. Why should $L_g < L_d$ on any particular iteration indicate a better generator? This could also be due to initialization, especially at the beginning of training.
3. **Grid Coarseness**: How does the coarseness of the grid from which $t, x$ are sampled affect the performance of the GANs over other methods?

### Minor Comments

1. **Notation Confusion**: The notation in section 4 is confusing in places: $G(x)$ and $\Psi_{\theta}(x)$ are used somewhat interchangeably; in lines 179-181, $L_g$ and $L_d$ are not defined.
2. **Legend in Figure 2**: In Figure 2, a legend is not required in every panel.
3. **Figure 3 Identification**: In Figure 3, the dotted lines in (a) and (b) are not identified in the legend; the colorbars have no label in (c) and (d).

### Limitations

It would be beneficial if the discussion could address the following issues:

1. **Interpretability of Generator**: While the discriminator is not interpretable, the generator has potentially the same issues. What scientific insight is gained from an (effectively) black box generator modeling a flow equation?
2. **Computational Costs and Efficiency**: While the GANs do provide an improvement over existing methods in terms of performance, it would be beneficial to have an idea of the associated computational costs, sample efficiency, and training times compared to the other methods.
3. **Scalability**: How does this method scale with the number of dynamical variables, the size of the coarseness of the $t, x$ grid?

### Rating

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one area, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

The expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation while elaborating on the original critique points and incorporating new observations and suggestions for improvement.

reviews.text:
## Summary

The paper presents a novel method, Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN), which leverages GAN-based adversarial training to “learn” the loss function for Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to solve differential equations. This approach is unsupervised, meaning it does not require solutions to the equations for training.

## Strengths

- **Novel Approach**: The paper provides a novel method to solve differential equations using GANs in an unsupervised training setup, which is a significant departure from traditional methods.
- **Improved Performance**: The results show that DEQGAN improves performance on differential equation tasks by multiple orders of magnitude compared to classical PINNs using pre-specified loss functions.
- **Effective Methods for Stable Training**: The authors integrate effective methods for stable training of GANs, such as instance noise and residual monitoring, which are crucial for the success of their approach.
- **Good Paper Presentation**: The paper presentation is good, with a lot of details provided for the experiments in the Appendix, making it easier to understand and replicate the work.

## Weaknesses

- **Mode Collapsing Discussion**: There is no discussion on mode collapsing, a common issue in GANs. Given that this is a different application of GANs, it would be beneficial to discuss whether the proposed GAN can provide different solutions for some equations and how mode collapsing might affect the results.
- **Theoretical Analysis**: The work lacks a theoretical analysis or guarantees, which are generally challenging for GANs but would be much appreciated for providing deeper insights into the method's behavior and limitations.

## Questions and Suggestions

- **Ablation Study**: It would be beneficial to see an ablation study that examines the effectiveness of the “tricks” used to train the GAN effectively. Some of these methods might not be necessary, and understanding their impact could refine the approach.
- **Mode Collapsing Comments**: As mentioned, comments on mode collapsing would be appreciated. Understanding how DEQGAN handles or avoids mode collapsing could provide insights into its reliability and versatility in solving differential equations.

## Limitations and Impact

- **Addressing Limitations**: The authors have addressed the limitations in the Conclusions section. The work is not applicable for negative societal impact, given its focus on solving differential equations, which is a fundamental task in various scientific and engineering fields.

## Rating

7: Accept - The paper is technically solid, with high impact on at least one sub-area (machine learning and differential equations), or moderate-to-high impact on more than one area. The evaluation is good, resources and reproducibility are considered, and there are no unaddressed ethical considerations.

The expanded review maintains the same decision/rating while elaborating on the original critique points and adding related observations. It uses specific details from the manuscript to support the points and reads naturally and fluently with the new content integrated seamlessly.

reviews.summary: This work proposes Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN) for solving differential equations using generative adversarial networks (GAN) where the generator learns to output the solution and the discriminator tries to learn a good loss function. The authors compare DEQGAN against classical PINNs over twelve ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs). The experimental results show that DEQGAN can achieve multiple orders of magnitude lower L2 error than PINNs.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: the paper is well-written and easy to follow. DEQGAN demonstrates superior empirical results compared to classical PINNs. The fact that DEQGAN doesn't depend on predefined distance such as L1, L2 can be useful in some problems.

Weakness:
1. The Related Work section misses some important discussion [1, 2]. The authors should discuss the relationship to works and place the contribution of DEQGAN in the context of prior works. For example, SA-PINNs [1] can also be viewed as learning a loss function.
2. The authors state that the L2 loss lacks theoretical justification but miss the important explanation on why and when L2 loss can be problematic. Furthermore, DEQGAN do not have a theoretical justification of the learned loss function either.
3. The loss function in DEQGAN is borrowed from generative adversarial network (GAN). The original GAN work designs its loss function such that generator is minimizing Jensen-Shannon divergence between the generated distribution and target distribution given optimal discriminator dynamics. However, the Jensen-Shannon divergence doesn't make sense in DEQGAN because the problem is to solve a differential equation with unique solution. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing the divergence between two Gaussians will just be minimizing the L2 loss. I do not see why a generative model is needed here.
4. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
5. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.

[1] McClenny, Levi, and Ulisses Braga-Neto. "Self-adaptive physics-informed neural networks using a soft attention mechanism." arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.04544 (2020).
[2] Daw, Arka, M. Maruf, and Anuj Karpatne. "PID-GAN: A GAN Framework based on a Physics-informed Discriminator for Uncertainty Quantification with Physics." Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining. 2021.
reviews.questions:
1. The authors motivate the need of learning loss function by saying that L2,L1 loss functions lack theoretical justification. However, the author challenges the standard practice in physics-informed learning without explaining why and when L1, L2 loss functions can be bad choices. If L2 loss is problematic, how is the learned loss function better than L2 loss? Can the authors provide theoretical justification for it?
2. How fast is DEQGAN compared to PINNs and numerical solvers? Can authors provide time complexity comparison against PINNs and its improved variants such as SA-PINNs?
3. Only one specific artificial initial condition is used for each equation. How will DEQGAN perform if the initial condition is a random function sampled from a random field?
reviews.limitations:
1. Lack of theoretical justification of the loss function and how the disciminator can learn a better objective function.
2. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.
3. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
reviews.rating: 3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
## Review

The authors propose Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN), a novel method for solving differential equations using generative adversarial networks (GANs). DEQGAN leverages a generator to output the solution and a discriminator to learn a suitable loss function. The authors compare DEQGAN with classical Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) on twelve ordinary and partial differential equations, demonstrating that DEQGAN achieves significantly lower mean squared errors.

### Strengths

The paper is well-written, easy to follow, and presents compelling empirical results. DEQGAN's ability to outperform classical PINNs is noteworthy, particularly in cases where the latter struggle with varied performance. The fact that DEQGAN does not rely on predefined distances like L1 or L2 loss functions can be advantageous in certain problems.

### Weaknesses

1. **Related Work**: The Related Work section could be more comprehensive. For instance, it misses discussions on Self-Adaptive PINNs (SA-PINNs) [1] and PID-GAN [2], which also explore learning loss functions or uncertainty quantification. A more detailed comparison with these works would help contextualize DEQGAN's contributions.

2. **Theoretical Justification**: The authors argue that L2 loss lacks theoretical justification but fail to provide a thorough explanation of why and when L2 loss can be problematic. Moreover, DEQGAN's learned loss function also lacks theoretical justification. This omission is crucial as it leaves the reader wondering about the reliability and generalizability of DEQGAN.

3. **Loss Function Design**: The loss function in DEQGAN is borrowed from GANs, which is designed to minimize the Jensen-Shannon divergence between the generated and target distributions under optimal discriminator dynamics. However, this divergence does not directly apply to solving differential equations with unique solutions. Even with Gaussian instance noise, the problem reduces to minimizing the L2 loss. The necessity of a generative model in this context is not clearly justified.

4. **Experimental Equations and Initial Conditions**: The equations considered are artificial and only one specific initial condition is used for each equation. This raises concerns that the authors may have selected initial conditions that favor DEQGAN's performance. It would be more convincing to test DEQGAN with a variety of initial conditions and more diverse equations.

5. **Training Instability**: DEQGAN suffers from training instability, requiring multiple runs and filtering out poor performances. This instability is a significant drawback, especially when compared to more robust methods like PINNs.

### Questions

1. **Theoretical Justification of Loss Function**: The authors motivate the need to learn a loss function by stating that L2 and L1 losses lack theoretical justification. However, they do not explain why and when these losses are bad choices. If L2 loss is problematic, how is the learned loss function better? Can the authors provide a theoretical justification for it?

2. **Computational Efficiency**: How does DEQGAN compare to PINNs and numerical solvers in terms of computational speed? A time complexity comparison against PINNs and its improved variants like SA-PINNs would be valuable.

3. **Performance with Random Initial Conditions**: How will DEQGAN perform if the initial condition is a random function sampled from a random field? The current experiments use specific artificial initial conditions, which might not represent real-world scenarios accurately.

### Limitations

1. **Lack of Theoretical Justification**: The loss function learned by DEQGAN lacks theoretical justification, and it is unclear how the discriminator learns a better objective function.

2. **Training Instability**: DEQGAN has a training instability issue, necessitating multiple runs and filtering out poor performances.

3. **Artificial Equations and Initial Conditions**: The equations considered are artificial, and only one specific initial condition is used for each, potentially biasing the results in favor of DEQGAN.

### Rating

3: Reject

The paper has technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility, and incompletely addressed ethical considerations. While DEQGAN shows promise, its limitations and the lack of thorough justification for several design choices are significant enough to warrant a rejection.

## References

[1] McClenny, Levi, and Ulisses Braga-Neto. "Self-adaptive physics-informed neural networks using a soft attention mechanism." arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.04544 (2020).

[2] Daw, Arka, M. Maruf, and Anuj Karpatne. "PID-GAN: A GAN Framework based on a Physics-informed Discriminator for Uncertainty Quantification with Physics." Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining. 2021.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This work proposes Differential Equation GAN (DEQGAN) for solving differential equations using generative adversarial networks (GAN) where the generator learns to output the solution and the discriminator tries to learn a good loss function. The authors compare DEQGAN against classical PINNs over twelve ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs). The experimental results show that DEQGAN can achieve multiple orders of magnitude lower L2 error than PINNs.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: the paper is well-written and easy to follow. DEQGAN demonstrates superior empirical results compared to classical PINNs. The fact that DEQGAN doesn't depend on predefined distance such as L1, L2 can be useful in some problems.

Weakness:
1. The Related Work section misses some important discussion [1, 2]. The authors should discuss the relationship to works and place the contribution of DEQGAN in the context of prior works. For example, SA-PINNs [1] can also be viewed as learning a loss function.
2. The authors state that the L2 loss lacks theoretical justification but miss the important explanation on why and when L2 loss can be problematic. Furthermore, DEQGAN do not have a theoretical justification of the learned loss function either.
3. The loss function in DEQGAN is borrowed from generative adversarial network (GAN). The original GAN work designs its loss function such that generator is minimizing Jensen-Shannon divergence between the generated distribution and target distribution given optimal discriminator dynamics. However, the Jensen-Shannon divergence doesn't make sense in DEQGAN because the problem is to solve a differential equation with unique solution. Even with Gaussian instance noise, minimizing the divergence between two Gaussians will just be minimizing the L2 loss. I do not see why a generative model is needed here.
4. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.
5. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.

[1] McClenny, Levi, and Ulisses Braga-Neto. "Self-adaptive physics-informed neural networks using a soft attention mechanism." arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.04544 (2020).
[2] Daw, Arka, M. Maruf, and Anuj Karpatne. "PID-GAN: A GAN Framework based on a Physics-informed Discriminator for Uncertainty Quantification with Physics." Proceedings of the 27th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery & Data Mining. 2021.

Questions:
1. The authors motivate the need of learning loss function by saying that L2,L1 loss functions lack theoretical justification. However, the author challenges the standard practice in physics-informed learning without explaining why and when L1, L2 loss functions can be bad choices. If L2 loss is problematic, how is the learned loss function better than L2 loss? Can the authors provide theoretical justification for it?
2. How fast is DEQGAN compared to PINNs and numerical solvers? Can authors provide time complexity comparison against PINNs and its improved variants such as SA-PINNs?
3. Only one specific artificial initial condition is used for each equation. How will DEQGAN perform if the initial condition is a random function sampled from a random field?

Limitations:
1. Lack of theoretical justification of the loss function and how the disciminator can learn a better objective function.
2. DEQGAN has training instability issue. To solve one equation, it needs to repeat runs multiple times and filters out the ones with poor performances.
3. The equations considered in the experiments are artificial. Only one specific initial condition is used for each equation, which raise the concern that the authors may pick the initial conditions to put DEQGAN in advantage in the experimental results.

Rating:
3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 3: Reject: For instance, a paper with technical flaws, weak evaluation, inadequate reproducibility and incompletely addressed ethical considerations.

title: ISAAC Newton: Input-based Approximate Curvature for Newton's Method

reviews.summary: The paper addresses the problem of using second-order information in large-scale machine learning tasks (e.g. training neural networks). It proposes a Kronecker-factored Approximate Curvature (KFAC)-approximated, Tikhonov regularized Gauss-Newton (GN) update step. Instead of a single regularization parameter ($\lambda$), the update step in the paper uses two independent regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$). The paper relates different settings of $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_g$ to other algorithms (i.e. classical GN and gradient descent). It shows that allowing $\lambda_g$ grows to infinity gives a computationally efficient update step. It provides empirical results on their proposed method.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The update step $\xi^*$ is interesting and practical. The paper provides experiments to show that using $\xi^*$ can lead to lower computation time without sacrificing performance. Experiments are thorough and tested on some fairly large neural network architectures.
2. The update step $\xi$ is a nice, compact way to describe an algorithm for training neural networks. The analysis of $\xi$ provides various ways of interpreting the algorithm in terms of different regularization parameter settings. The properties of $\xi$ are interesting. The analysis is relatively thorough (e.g. showing that any combination of $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$ will still result in a descent direction).
3. Paper is well-written and clear. 

Weaknesses
1. Some of the experiments are not benchmarked to SOTA (which is acknowledged by the paper). This might affect people's willingness to adopt the method in practice.
reviews.questions:
1. Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Typo?
2. Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?
reviews.limitations: This work does not appear to me to have negative social impact.

Strengths And Weaknesses:

reviews.text:
## Summary

The paper introduces ISAAC Newton, a novel method that conditions the gradient using selected second-order information and has an asymptotically vanishing computational overhead. The proposed method, ISAAC, is based on a Kronecker-factored Approximate Curvature (KFAC)-approximated, Tikhonov regularized Gauss-Newton (GN) update step. Instead of a single regularization parameter ($\lambda$), the update step uses two independent regularization parameters ($\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$). The paper relates different settings of $\lambda_a$ and $\lambda_g$ to other algorithms, such as classical GN and gradient descent. It shows that allowing $\lambda_g$ to grow to infinity gives a computationally efficient update step. The paper provides empirical results on the proposed method, demonstrating its effectiveness in various settings.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

### Strengths

1. **Innovative Update Step**: The update step $\xi^*$ is interesting and practical. The paper provides experiments to show that using $\xi^*$ can lead to lower computation time without sacrificing performance. Experiments are thorough and tested on some fairly large neural network architectures.
2. **Compact Algorithm Description**: The update step $\xi$ is a nice, compact way to describe an algorithm for training neural networks. The analysis of $\xi$ provides various ways of interpreting the algorithm in terms of different regularization parameter settings. The properties of $\xi$ are interesting. The analysis is relatively thorough (e.g., showing that any combination of $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$ will still result in a descent direction).
3. **Clear Writing**: The paper is well-written and clear.

### Weaknesses

1. **Limited Comparison to SOTA**: Some of the experiments are not benchmarked to State-of-the-Art (SOTA), which might affect people's willingness to adopt the method in practice.

## Questions

1. **Typo in Equation Reference**: Line 70 refers to $H$ in Equation 6. Is this a typo?
2. **Interpretation of Preconditioner**: Is there an intuitive interpretation of the preconditioner $M$ in Remark 2? Does this preconditioning help in terms of reducing the number of iterations needed to reach a solution?

## Limitations

This work does not appear to have negative social impact.

## Rating

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one area, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

## Expanded Review

The paper presents a novel approach to incorporating second-order information into the training of large-scale machine learning models, specifically neural networks. The proposed method, ISAAC, leverages a Kronecker-factored approximation of the curvature matrix to achieve an efficient update step. This update step is based on a Tikhonov regularized Gauss-Newton update, which is modified to include two independent regularization parameters, $\lambda_g$ and $\lambda_a$. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the properties of the update step, including its relationship to other optimization algorithms and its descent direction properties.

One of the key contributions of the paper is the introduction of the efficient update step $\xi^*$, which is achieved by allowing $\lambda_g$ to grow to infinity. This results in a computationally efficient update step that can be computed with a vanishing overhead. The authors provide empirical evidence to support the effectiveness of $\xi^*$, demonstrating its performance on various benchmark problems.

The paper also provides a detailed analysis of the theoretical properties of the update step, including its asymptotic behavior and its relationship to other optimization algorithms. The authors show that the update step $\xi$ is a descent direction, regardless of the choice of regularization parameters. This is an important result, as it ensures that the update step will always improve the objective function.

The experiments provided in the paper are thorough and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. However, as noted in the original review, some of the experiments are not benchmarked to SOTA, which might affect people's willingness to adopt the method in practice. Nevertheless, the results presented in the paper are promising and suggest that ISAAC has the potential to be a competitive optimization algorithm for large-scale machine learning tasks.

In terms of the implementation, the authors provide a PyTorch implementation of the proposed method, which is a useful resource for researchers and practitioners. The implementation is well-documented and easy to follow, making it accessible to a wide range of users.

Overall, the paper presents a novel and effective approach to incorporating second-order information into the training of large-scale machine learning models. The proposed method, ISAAC, has the potential to be a competitive optimization algorithm for a wide range of applications, and the authors provide a thorough analysis of its theoretical and empirical properties.

The decision to accept this paper is based on its technical solidity, high impact on at least one sub-area, and good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations. The expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation as the original review, and all original critique points are preserved and remain unchanged. New sentences and paragraphs have been added to elaborate on existing points and add related observations, and the expansion uses specific details from the manuscript to support the points. The review reads naturally and fluently with the new content integrated seamlessly.

reviews.summary:
This paper proposed a KFAC variant to improve the computation efficiency. It introduces two Tikhonov regularization terms into KFAC, and makes gradient conditioning more efficient by force $\lambda_g$ go to infinity. The authors present a theoretical guarantee that the optimization still goes in the right direction even with such an adaptation. The authors also show ISAAC is more computation efficient in small-batch stochastic regimes.

However, I feel contributions in this paper are rather limited, and some motivations might need further justification.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

The paper is well written. Preliminaries and methods are clearly explained. 

Weaknesses:

1, Contributions are limited. Reducing the compute costs of KFAC is a good problem. However, I am not convinced that separating the regularization term and using a large regularization is a novel and effective solution. It only makes the optimizer behave more and more like SGD. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, small $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to KFAC, while large $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to vanilla gradient descent. 

2, Empirical results need to be enhanced. The authors use several empirical results to support the claim that ISAAC still behaves like KFAC even though using a strong regularization factor. However, I do not think current results are strong enough to support the claim. The issues in the empirical evaluations also make me doubt the contributions in the paper. 

- 2.1, The author should provide more baselines (Adam, SGD with momentum) so that we can really have a fair evaluation. 

- 2.2, For Figure 1, my understanding is that the authors are trying to show ISAAC still obtains good results (loss and accuracy) even with large regularization. If so, I do not think it is necessary. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, larger regularization makes ISAAC behave more like SGD. Therefore, getting similar loss and accuracy is no surprise.
    
- 2.3, For Figure 2, there are several issues. First, hyper parameters are not fully revealed. For instance, what is the weight decay for each optimizer since weight decays large affects training convergence?  Second, only the training curve is reported. The author really should present both a training and validation curve. In many cases, we can have faster convergence on training dataset, but not for validation dataset. 

- 2.4, For Figure 6, the reported accuracy is obviously lower compared to results we usually have on CIFAR-10. The reason might be that the authors use SGD without momentum. However, I am not convinced under such a setting. SGD with momentum is a default setting in many training tasks. Without momentum, I do not think the results will provide meaningful comparisons and conclusions.

3, Training with small batches should be further justified. I understand the authors stress the compute benefits of ISAAC in a small-batch regime (Theorem 3-ii). However, considering more general settings, the authors should well justify it: is it worth accelerating the training using small batches given the fact that large batches can also improve convergence performance even using SGD. Therefore, I think not only should the author provide results in small-batch regimes, training using large batches should also be evaluated and compared with other baselines.
reviews.questions: See my comments above.
reviews.limitations: No
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.text:
The paper proposes a variant of the Kronecker-factored approximate curvature (K-FAC) method, called ISAAC Newton, which aims to improve computational efficiency by introducing two Tikhonov regularization terms. The authors present a theoretical guarantee that the optimization still proceeds in the right direction even with the adaptation of setting $\lambda_g$ to infinity. They also demonstrate that ISAAC is more computationally efficient in small-batch stochastic regimes.

However, I feel that the contributions in this paper are rather limited, and some motivations might need further justification. The problem of reducing the computational costs of K-FAC is well-defined, but I am not convinced that separating the regularization term and using a large regularization is a novel and effective solution. As the authors stated in Theorem 1, small $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to K-FAC, while large $\lambda_a, \lambda_g$ leads to vanilla gradient descent. This suggests that the proposed method may not offer significant improvements over existing methods.

The empirical results need to be enhanced to support the claim that ISAAC still behaves like K-FAC even when using a strong regularization factor. The authors should provide more baselines, such as Adam and SGD with momentum, to ensure a fair evaluation. For instance, in Figure 1, the authors only show the training loss and accuracy, but not the validation loss and accuracy. This is a crucial omission, as it is well-known that methods can have faster convergence on the training dataset but not on the validation dataset.

Furthermore, the hyperparameter settings for each optimizer are not fully revealed. For example, the weight decay for each optimizer is not specified, which can significantly affect training convergence. The authors should provide more details about the hyperparameter tuning process to ensure that the results are reproducible.

In Figure 2, the authors report the training loss but not the validation loss. This omission makes it difficult to assess the generalization performance of the proposed method. Additionally, the reported accuracy in Figure 6 is lower than what is typically reported on CIFAR-10. The authors should provide more details about the experimental setting, such as the use of momentum in SGD.

The authors stress the computational benefits of ISAAC in a small-batch regime (Theorem 3-ii). However, considering more general settings, the authors should justify whether it is worth accelerating training using small batches given that large batches can also improve convergence performance even using SGD. Therefore, I think not only should the authors provide results in small-batch regimes, but training using large batches should also be evaluated and compared with other baselines.

In terms of limitations, the authors do not discuss potential negative societal impacts of their work. While the proposed method may have computational benefits, it is essential to consider its potential applications and whether they may have any negative consequences.

Overall, I believe that the paper has some technical merits, but its contributions are limited, and the empirical evaluations need to be enhanced. Therefore, I recommend a rating of 5: Borderline accept.

Rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.
reviews.questions: What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.

Questions:
What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Summary

The paper presents ISAAC Newton, a novel method that conditions the gradient using selected second-order information and has an asymptotically vanishing computational overhead. The authors show that it is possible to compute a good conditioner based on only the input to a respective layer without a substantial computational overhead. The proposed method allows effective training even in small-batch stochastic regimes, making it competitive to first-order as well as quasi-Newton methods.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

### Strengths

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation.
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

### Weaknesses

1. It is not clear what the main contribution of this paper is. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)?
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.

## Detailed Review

The authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect.

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation. For instance, in Figure 2, the authors demonstrate that ζζζ ∗ achieves similar performance to K-FAC, while being substantially cheaper to compute. Additionally, the authors show that ζζζ ∗ may be used for individual layers, while for other layers only the gradient ∇ is used, leading to improved performance.

## Intuition Behind Eq (21)

Equation (21) presents a particular pre-conditioning of ζζζ, since ζζζ ∗ = Mζζζ for M = 1/(bλg) \* ¯g¯g¯g⊤¯g¯g¯g. This equation can be interpreted as a modification of the original update direction ζζζ, where the term ¯g¯g¯g is removed, leading to a more efficient computation. The intuition behind this is that, in the limit of λg → ∞, the term ¯g¯g¯g becomes less important, and the update direction ζζζ ∗ remains a good approximation of the Gauss-Newton update direction.

## Comparison with Eq (13)

Compared to Eq (13), Eq (21) can be seen as a more efficient approximation of the Gauss-Newton matrix. While Eq (13) requires computing the Kronecker product of two matrices, Eq (21) only requires computing a single matrix product, leading to a reduced computational complexity.

## Rating

5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

## Expanded Decision

The expanded review supports the same acceptance/rejection recommendation as the original review. The paper presents a novel method for efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation, and the experiments demonstrate its effectiveness. However, the main contribution of the paper is not entirely clear, and some theoretical results appear trivial. Nevertheless, the paper's technical solidity and comprehensive experiments outweigh the reasons to reject it.

## Additional Comments

* The authors may want to consider providing more intuition behind the choice of the two-parameter damping mechanism.
* The special case mentioned in Remark 3 may need further justification for its effectiveness without $g^{\top}g$.
* The computational efficiency claim would be more convincing with a theoretical comparison with K-FAC.

## Conclusion

In conclusion, the paper presents a novel method for efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation. While the main contribution is not entirely clear, and some theoretical results appear trivial, the paper's technical solidity and comprehensive experiments make it a borderline accept. The authors may want to consider addressing the above comments to further strengthen their paper.

## References

[1] J. Martens and R. Grosse, “Optimizing neural networks with Kronecker-factored approximate curvature,” in International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2015.

[2] A. Botev, H. Ritter, and D. Barber, “Practical Gauss-Newton optimisation for deep learning,” in International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2017.

[3] F. Dangel, F. Kunstner, and P. Hennig, “Backpack: Packing more into backprop,” in International Conference on Learning Representations, 2020.

## Checklist

1. For all authors...

(a) Do the main claims made in the abstract and introduction accurately reflect the paper’s contributions and scope? [Yes]

(b) Did you describe the limitations of your work? [Yes]

(c) Did you discuss any potential negative societal impacts of your work? [N/A]

(d) Have you read the ethics review guidelines and ensured that your paper conforms to them? [Yes]

2. If you are including theoretical results...

(a) Did you state the full set of assumptions of all theoretical results? [Yes]

(b) Did you include complete proofs of all theoretical results? [Yes]

3. If you ran experiments...

(a) Did you include the code, data, and instructions needed to reproduce the main experimental results (either in the supplemental material or as a URL)? [Yes] 

(b) Did you specify all the training details (e.g., data splits, hyperparameters, how they were chosen)? [Yes]

(c) Did you report error bars (e.g., with respect to the random seed after running experiments multiple times)? [Yes]

(d) Did you include the total amount of compute and the type of resources used (e.g., type of GPUs, internal cluster, or cloud provider)? [Yes]

4. If you are using existing assets (e.g., code, data, models) or curating/releasing new assets...

(a) If your work uses existing assets, did you cite the creators? [Yes]

(b) Did you mention the license of the assets? [N/A]

(c) Did you include any new assets either in the supplemental material or as a URL? [N/A]

(d) Did you discuss whether and how consent was obtained from people whose data you’re using/curating? [N/A]

(e) Did you discuss whether the data you are using/curating contains personally identifiable information or offensive content? [N/A]

5. If you used crowdsourcing or conducted research with human subjects...

(a) Did you include the full text of instructions given to participants and screenshots, if applicable? [N/A]

(b) Did you describe any potential participant risks, with links to Institutional Review Board (IRB) approvals, if applicable? [N/A]

(c) Did you include the estimated hourly wage paid to participants and the total amount spent on participant compensation? [N/A]

Summary:
In unconstrained optimisation, Newton's method uses inverse Hessian to determine the step size of the update. When applied to deep learning, the sheer size of the Hessian matrix makes Newton's method intractable. Among existing attempts to address this challenge, K-FAC is a promising approach. In practice, Tikhonov regularization/damping is used to avoid divergence.

In this paper, the authors introduced an extension to the standard Tikhonov damping for K-FAC. The main idea is to use two independent damping parameters (hyper-parameters for regularisation) instead of one damping parameter, one for the gradient factor and one for the activity factor, as shown in Equation (17). The authors explored special cases where the two parameters are either 0 or infinity. They also discussed the special case of setting one parameter to infinity, effectively removing the gradient factor in K-FAC. One major claim was that this activity-only form has an advantage in reduced computational complexity, although I don't find a theoretical comparison with K-FAC in this aspect. 

The claim for computational efficiency was mostly supported via empirical evaluation.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. The paper addresses an important problem: efficient approximation of Gaussian-Newton approximation
2. The exposition is mostly clear, especially if you are already familiar with the existing literature.
3. The experiments seem comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

1. It is not clear what is the main contribution of this paper. Is it the two-parameter damping mechanism (What advantage do we gain)? Or is it the special case mentioned in Remark 3 (Why this is a still good approximation without $g^{\top}g$)? 
2. Many theoretical results presented look rather trivial.

Questions:
What is the intuition behind Eq (21)? Compared with Eq (13), why is it a good approximation?

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Regularization parameters is crucial for second-order type methods.  The authors first approximate the GGN matrix as a kroncker product of two matrices, and then view two regulation parameters independently. By letting the two regularization parameters vanish or go to infinity, one can obtain new directions and therefore new methods. The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $g$.  

Weaknesses:

This paper should be polished to make it easier to read. 

Some related literatures on second-order methods should be mentioned, such as Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4]. 

Some theoretical properties is natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result.

It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo[1], KBFGS[2], NG+[3] and SENG[4].

reviews.text:
## Summary

Inspired by the KFAC approximation and the Tikhonov regularization of the Newton method, this paper proposes a regularized K-FAC approximated Gauss-Newton type method called ISAAC. The proposed method is based on only the input to a respective layer, therefore does not suffer from a substantial computational overhead. Numerical experiments show the advantages of the proposed method compared to both first-order type and second-order type methods.

## Strengths And Weaknesses

### Strengths

*   Regularization parameters are crucial for second-order type methods. The authors first approximate the GGN matrix as a Kronecker product of two matrices, and then view two regularization parameters independently. By letting the two regularization parameters vanish or go to infinity, one can obtain new directions and therefore new methods. The proposed method is more efficient than KFAC since only one matrix should be inverted and no need extra propagation to compute $\bar{g}$.
*   The paper provides a detailed analysis of the proposed method, including its theoretical properties and empirical performance.

### Weaknesses

*   This paper should be polished to make it easier to read. Specifically, the writing can be improved to make it more clear and concise.
*   Some related literatures on second-order methods should be mentioned, such as Shampoo [1], KBFGS [2], NG+ [3], and SENG [4]. These methods provide a broader context for the proposed method and demonstrate its potential advantages.
*   Some theoretical properties are natural and trivial. It is better to formalize an algorithm and give a convergence result. For example, the authors could provide a formal proof of the convergence of the proposed method.
*   It is better to add some large numerical examples, such as Resnet50 with Imagenet-1k and make a comparison with other second-order methods Shampoo [1], KBFGS [2], NG+ [3], and SENG [4]. This would help to demonstrate the practical advantages of the proposed method.

## Detailed Comments

*   Line 60: change [14],[15] to [14,15]. It is better to put multiple references in the same brackets. Please check other places.
*   It is better to add the discussion between GGN matrix and Fisher matrix, which can refer to [1,2] and related literatures. The GGN matrix and Fisher matrix are closely related, and a discussion of their relationship would provide additional context for the proposed method.
*   It is better to add a left bracket after $\eta$ and a right bracket before the gradient in eq.(16). Please also add brackets in a suitable place in eq. (17). This would improve the clarity of the equations.
*   In eq.(19), the gradient should be $\mathrm{g}^\top a /b$ where the $b$ is missed. In eq.(19), the authors use the same batch to approximate the GGN matrix and compute the gradient. Can the batches for approximation of the GGN and the computation of the gradient be different? In that case, what the related theoretical properties will be? This is an important consideration, as the choice of batch can affect the performance of the method.
*   When the objective function is convex, the GGN matrix is positive semi-definite matrix. Adding a regularization parameter in GGN, It is easy to show that $\zeta$ is an ascent direction, which means Theorem 2 is trivial. This is an important consideration, as it highlights the importance of careful analysis of the theoretical properties of the proposed method.
*   The authors consider the fully-connected neural network in this paper. I believe this method can be extended to convolutional neural networks. And in numerical part, the authors have done some experiments. I wonder can we obtain some explanation from theory for this case? This would be an important contribution, as convolutional neural networks are widely used in practice.
*   For the Kronecker approximation of the GGN matrix, $\mathrm{a}\mathrm{a}^\top$ is more important than $\overline{g}^\top\overline{g}$ part. One reason is that each value of the matrix $\overline{g}^\top\overline{g}$ is very small so that the $\lambda_g$ domains. I have noticed one related work recently. The work is named NG+ [3]. This is an interesting observation, and it highlights the importance of careful consideration of the Kronecker approximation.

## Limitations

The paper has several limitations. First, the writing can be improved to make it more clear and concise. Second, the paper could benefit from a more detailed analysis of the theoretical properties of the proposed method. Finally, the paper could be strengthened by the inclusion of more numerical experiments, including larger-scale experiments.

## Rating

5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

The expanded review leads to the same acceptance/rejection recommendation as the original review. The paper proposes a novel and interesting method, and the authors have made a good effort to analyze its theoretical properties and empirical performance. However, there are some areas for improvement, including the writing, the analysis of theoretical properties, and the inclusion of more numerical experiments.

title: LeRaC: Learning Rate Curriculum

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to modify neural net training by applying a different learning rate to each layer: the deeper layers are assigned a lower learning rate that progressively grows to match the first layer learning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is clear, the approach is simple and can be implemented easily. The experiments shows better generalization when adopting the proposed learning rate schedule. The paper could be improved on two main axes:
(i) presenting the approach as an optimization method seem more appropriate than the curriculum learning angle, (ii) more analysis and justifications of why the method works seem necessary.
Overall, I feel that the paper does not explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule is different from the effective learning rates reached by different settings of adaptive optimizers (momentum, Adadelta, Adam, SM3...).
reviews.questions:
== Optimization vs curriculum learning ==

The proposed method uses a different learning rate per layer. This endeavor has been widely explored in the literature on optimization of neural nets while the name curriculum learning is usually employed for methods modifying the ordering of the examples during training. I agree that the name curriculum could make sense in your context but the paper needs to spend more time on previous work on optimization. This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).

To justify your approach it seems necessary to compare your method at least with Adam and report the effective average learning rate (e.g. see Zeiler 2012's definition) per layer for both methods and how their parameters control the differentiation of learning rates across layers during training. The fact that your method is used in conjunction with Adam (or variants) could mean that either (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters or (ii) your method's benefit comes from schedules unreachable by Adam. It seems very important to explore both methods' parameters to distinguish these two cases.

== Benefit is faster training or better generalization? ==

Modifying the training algorithm requires reporting training curves with *both* validation and training loss over time. The paper does not report any training loss which left us wonder if the generalization benefit comes from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) versus a better generalization trajectory (reaching a lower validation loss at the same training loss). 

== Schedule Tuning ==

Could you explain how the exponential scheduler of Eq. 8 has been selected? Is it the only option that you tried? How is the training speed affected by the choice of the additional hyperparameters (c, \nu^{(0)}_n, k)? In particular, compared to a robust baseline like Adam, would most hyperparameter choices yield faster or slower training? I do not understand how c can be set to 10 without validation.

== Number of training steps ==

The experimental section should spend more time describing how the number of training steps was selected. In which cases the baselines would reach the same or better validation loss with more steps? E.g. Figure 1.d. seems to suggest that better validation loss with CBS can be reached with more training. This improvement does not seem to agree with the sentence "all models are trained with early stopping", does it?

== Justification of the noise argument ==

Adaptive methods (Adam etc) justify higher learning rates for first layers with a curvature argument: in laymans term, to get the same impact on the output the first layers' updates require a larger learning rates than the last ones. Adadelta (Zeiler 2012) also mentions the larger learning rate in light of the vanishing gradient effect (Bengio 2014). Your motivation (from [21]) is to reduce the effect of "noise due to untrained parameters". This argument is unclear to the reader and it would help if you could find a way to measure/characterize that "noise" and show how your method reduces its effect.
reviews.limitations: This work does not raises concerns on societal impact.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes to modify neural net training by applying a different learning rate to each layer: the deeper layers are assigned a lower learning rate that progressively grows to match the first layer learning rate.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is clear, the approach is simple and can be implemented easily. The experiments shows better generalization when adopting the proposed learning rate schedule. The paper could be improved on two main axes:
(i) presenting the approach as an optimization method seem more appropriate than the curriculum learning angle, (ii) more analysis and justifications of why the method works seem necessary.
Overall, I feel that the paper does not explain how/if the proposed differentiated learning rate schedule is different from the effective learning rates reached by different settings of adaptive optimizers (momentum, Adadelta, Adam, SM3...).

Questions:
== Optimization vs curriculum learning ==

The proposed method uses a different learning rate per layer. This endeavor has been widely explored in the literature on optimization of neural nets while the name curriculum learning is usually employed for methods modifying the ordering of the examples during training. I agree that the name curriculum could make sense in your context but the paper needs to spend more time on previous work on optimization. This means expanding the related work section and the empirical analysis.

The Adadelta paper (Zeiler 2012) observes the benefit to use different learning rates for different layers and Adadelta results in "effective learning rates [...] are larger for the lower layers of the network and much
smaller for the top layer at the beginning of training." Similar observations can be made for other adaptive learning rate algorithms like Adagrad, Adam, Adafactor, Adamax and AdamW or even later algorithms like e.g. SM3 (Memory-Efficient Adaptive Optimization, NeurIPS'19, see Figure 1.).

To justify your approach it seems necessary to compare your method at least with Adam and report the effective average learning rate (e.g. see Zeiler 2012's definition) per layer for both methods and how their parameters control the differentiation of learning rates across layers during training. The fact that your method is used in conjunction with Adam (or variants) could mean that either (i) the same type of learning rate schedule could be attained by changing Adam's parameters or (ii) your method's benefit comes from schedules unreachable by Adam. It seems very important to explore both methods' parameters to distinguish these two cases.

== Benefit is faster training or better generalization? ==

Modifying the training algorithm requires reporting training curves with *both* validation and training loss over time. The paper does not report any training loss which left us wonder if the generalization benefit comes from better optimization (lower training and validation loss) versus a better generalization trajectory (reaching a lower validation loss at the same training loss). 

== Schedule Tuning ==

Could you explain how the exponential scheduler of Eq. 8 has been selected? Is it the only option that you tried? How is the training speed affected by the choice of the additional hyperparameters (c, \nu^{(0)}_n, k)? In particular, compared to a robust baseline like Adam, would most hyperparameter choices yield faster or slower training? I do not understand how c can be set to 10 without validation.

== Number of training steps ==

The experimental section should spend more time describing how the number of training steps was selected. In which cases the baselines would reach the same or better validation loss with more steps? E.g. Figure 1.d. seems to suggest that better validation loss with CBS can be reached with more training. This improvement does not seem to agree with the sentence "all models are trained with early stopping", does it?

== Justification of the noise argument ==

Adaptive methods (Adam etc) justify higher learning rates for first layers with a curvature argument: in laymans term, to get the same impact on the output the first layers' updates require a larger learning rates than the last ones. Adadelta (Zeiler 2012) also mentions the larger learning rate in light of the vanishing gradient effect (Bengio 2014). Your motivation (from [21]) is to reduce the effect of "noise due to untrained parameters". This argument is unclear to the reader and it would help if you could find a way to measure/characterize that "noise" and show how your method reduces its effect.

Limitations:
This work does not raises concerns on societal impact.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
